items.title: Email verification on registration
items.description: Verify email before activating account. Prevents spam registrations. Needs SMTP configuration.

items.title: Mobile apps via Capacitor (iOS + Android)
items.description:
Native iOS and Android apps via Capacitor — the same webview-wrapper model we already adopted with Tauri on desktop. The entire React codebase ships as-is; no rewrite, no dual UI maintenance.

Why Capacitor over React Native: RN would require rewriting every component (different primitives, layout engine, navigation), replacing Web Crypto (we need this for E2E — no native RN equivalent, needs react-native-quick-crypto), migrating IndexedDB key storage, and using react-native-webrtc for voice. Business logic shares at best ~30-50%, UI is from scratch, and we'd maintain two codebases forever. Capacitor gives us 100% code reuse and keeps the architectural story consistent with desktop.

Why not pure PWA: iOS PWAs work but there's no App Store presence (organic discovery, trust signal for enterprise), push notifications are second-class on iOS, and the 'is this a real app' perception problem is real for business users.

## Phase 1: Responsive redesign (~60% of the effort)

Has to happen regardless of packaging. Babelr is currently desktop-first with assumptions that break below ~768px.

- Server sidebar + channel sidebar collapse to slide-out drawers on narrow viewports
- Bottom nav bar (DMs / Channels / Friends / Notifications) for iOS-native feel
- Tap targets sized ≥44×44px per Apple/Google guidelines
- Remove hover-only affordances — already started prepping with the always-visible channel settings gear
- Message input handles on-screen keyboard gracefully (viewport resize aware, input stays above keyboard)
- Font sizes and spacing bumped for touch readability
- Swipe-to-reply, long-press-for-context-menu patterns replacing right-click
- Channel header compacts gracefully on narrow widths (breadcrumb collapses, topic truncates earlier)
- Settings panels become full-screen sheets instead of floating modals on mobile
- Test on real phone browsers throughout — the iOS webview has subtle layout quirks

## Phase 2: Capacitor wrap (~1 sitting)

- `npx cap init` in the monorepo, add iOS and Android platforms
- Point Capacitor at the existing Vite build output
- Update CORS + CSP for the `capacitor://` and `ionic://` schemes
- Test in iOS Simulator and Android Emulator
- Backend: detect Capacitor user agent so the client can pass through a native-origin header for any auth quirks

## Phase 3: Native plugin integration (~2-3 sittings)

- `@capacitor/push-notifications` — register device token on login, POST to a new `/devices` endpoint server-side, receive APNs/FCM payloads, show local notifications, deep-link to the relevant channel/DM on tap
- New server table `user_devices(id, actorId, platform, token, createdAt, lastSeenAt)` + routes to register/unregister
- Server-side push dispatcher: on new message matching a mention or DM, fan out to registered devices via APNs/FCM. Rate-limit per device.
- `@capacitor/preferences` or `@capacitor-community/sqlite` for E2E key storage — migrate away from IndexedDB which behaves differently in native webviews across uninstall/reinstall cycles
- `@capacitor/filesystem` + `@capacitor/camera` — attachment picker uses native file chooser and camera access, not web `<input type=file>`
- `@capacitor/haptics` — subtle haptic feedback on message send, long-press, errors
- `@capacitor/status-bar` + `@capacitor/splash-screen` — polish the native shell
- Biometric auth via `@capgo/capacitor-native-biometric` for quick unlock (optional, gated on settings)

## Phase 4: WebRTC on mobile

The web WebRTC APIs work in Capacitor's webview, but voice channels will need testing against the mobile permission model:
- iOS is strict about audio capture in background — need `UIBackgroundModes: audio` in Info.plist and CallKit integration for backgrounding
- Android needs `RECORD_AUDIO` permission + foreground service for ongoing calls
- Test against voice-channels-mvp end-to-end on both platforms before shipping

Bundle this phase with the voice-channels-mvp rollout.

## Phase 5: App store submission (~1 sitting each platform)

- Icons at every required size (Android adaptive icons, iOS full set)
- Screenshots at every required device class (iPhone 15, iPad, Android phone, tablet) — automate via Fastlane if possible
- App Store Connect listing: title, subtitle, description, keywords, privacy policy URL, support URL. Localize the listing using the i18n strings infrastructure — store listings in 16 languages is a real SEO advantage.
- Google Play listing: same deal
- Privacy manifest (iOS) — required since iOS 17, declare all tracking domains and APIs used. Babelr is actually well-positioned here because we don't track.
- Apple App Review: usually wants a demo account and a description of federation. Expect one round of back-and-forth clarifying the federated chat model.
- Google Play review: generally faster, less friction
- Set up CI for store builds (GitHub Actions + Fastlane) so we can ship updates without local Xcode setup

## Estimate

Probably 5-7 sittings total, weighted heavily toward the responsive redesign. The Capacitor wrap itself is nearly trivial. Push notification infra is the next-biggest chunk after responsive. Voice-on-mobile integration folds into the voice channels work.

## Open questions

- Paid developer accounts: Apple $99/year, Google $25 one-time. Budget item.
- Review risk: federated chat is unusual for mobile stores; Apple occasionally rejects things that look like 'social' without a moderation story. Our admin/moderation tooling should cover this but expect clarifying questions.
- Push translation: notifications shown on the lock screen — do we translate the message preview or show the original? Probably should honor the user's preferredLanguage setting and translate, matching the in-app behavior. Adds a server-side pre-translate step on fan-out.

items.title: Real-time speech translation in voice channels
items.description: Whisper STT + tone-preserving rewrite + TTS in the listener's language. Major bet — requires latency budget, model orchestration, GPU costs. Phase after voice-channels-mvp ships.

items.title: Federation interop validation against a second instance
items.description: Stand up a second Babelr deployment (and where applicable test against a Mastodon-compatible instance) and validate every federation path end-to-end: WebFinger lookup, Person actor resolution + ECDH key fetch, Group actor + Follow/Accept for server joins, Note delivery for public channel messages, targeted DM Create activities with E2E payloads, Read receipts, friend-request Follow/Accept/Undo, Delete tombstones, avatar icon round-trip, Group server logo icon round-trip. Currently every federated feature has only been tested against itself; the wire format may pass our own validation and still get rejected by stricter implementations. Highest-leverage launch blocker.

items.title: Calendars and events (user + server) — core
items.description: Shipped in commit 173c041. Core system: events + event_attendees tables, full CRUD + RSVP API, rrule.js recurrence expansion, per-event chat via auto-created OrderedCollection reusing the channel message pipeline, CreateEventModal / EventDetailPanel / EventsPanel UI, sidebar Calendar button, linked-channel deep-linking from server events. Both user and server scopes working. See follow-up items for federation, iCal export, monthly view, push notifications, per-instance RSVPs, and event-content translation.

items.title: Calendar event federation via AS2 Event + Join/Leave
items.description:
Federate events across Babelr instances using the native ActivityPub mechanisms. AS2 has a native Event object type in the core spec, so this piggybacks on the existing delivery queue + WebFinger + signature pipeline without inventing a new protocol.

Scope:
- serializeEvent() helper emitting AS2 Event JSON: type=Event, name, content, startTime, endTime, location, attributedTo, published, and babelr-ns custom fields for rrule (since AS2 doesn't standardize recurrence)
- On event create: broadcast a Create activity wrapping the Event, same pattern as broadcastCreate for Notes. For server events, deliver to all server followers (federation-aware). For user events, deliver to all attendees who are remote.
- On RSVP: send a Join activity with object=eventUri, actor=callerUri. The Undo(Join) pattern handles un-RSVP.
- Inbound handleCreate: when activity.object.type === 'Event', store it as a remote event row (ownerType=user|server based on attributedTo), attendees collection tracked as RSVPs from remote actors
- Inbound Join handler: record a remote RSVP against the local event if we're authoritative, or update the cached remote event's attendee list otherwise
- Remote-event visibility: remote events show up in local calendar views for users who follow the remote server or attend directly
- WebSocket push: events:new, events:updated, events:rsvp for the actor's subscribed calendars so the UI updates live

Depends on: federation-interop-test (to validate the federation paths end-to-end before trusting this new path).

items.title: iCal export feed per user and per server
items.description:
GET /events.ics endpoints that return a standards-compliant iCalendar feed of the caller's events (or a server's public events). Lets people subscribe from Google Calendar, Apple Calendar, Outlook, Thunderbird, etc. — their client polls the feed on its own schedule, no push needed.

Scope:
- GET /users/:username/events.ics — authenticated-token-based or public-feed-token feed of user events
- GET /servers/:serverId/events.ics — same for server events
- Use the `ics` npm package to generate the feed, or hand-roll (the format is not complex)
- Expand recurrences into VEVENT blocks OR emit a single VEVENT with RRULE (most clients handle RRULE natively — preferred)
- Include UID, DTSTART, DTEND, SUMMARY, DESCRIPTION, LOCATION, ORGANIZER, ATTENDEE entries
- Token-based auth for feed URLs so people can paste the URL into their calendar client without a login flow: generate a per-user feed token, stored in actors.properties, regenerable
- Server event feeds respect server visibility (public servers emit a public feed, private servers require the token)

High-leverage, low cost — once events federate, the iCal feed lets Babelr events show up next to work meetings in whatever calendar the user already lives in.

items.title: Month and week calendar views for EventsPanel
items.description:
Adds month-grid and week-timeline views alongside the existing Today/Tomorrow/This week/Later bucket list, plus a three-way toggle to switch between them.

Shipped:
- Three view modes: Agenda (existing bucket list, default), Week (Google Calendar shape with hour scale + day columns), Month (6-row × 7-col grid).
- Week view uses a 6am–midnight hour rail with events positioned absolutely at their start time and height proportional to duration. Today column highlighted.
- Month view renders 6 × 7 cells covering partial leading/trailing weeks. Up to 3 event summaries per cell with '+N more' overflow. Today cell highlighted; out-of-month cells dimmed.
- Navigation: prev/next arrows that shift by ±1 week or ±1 month, a Today button that jumps to the current date, and a range label showing the current window.
- Dynamic range management: week/month views pass explicit rangeStart/rangeEnd to useEvents based on the visible window, so past events and far-future events load when navigated to.
- Event translation continues to work across all three views via the existing useEventTranslation hook.
- ~270 lines of CSS for the grid and timeline layouts.

Explicitly deferred (polish follow-ups): drag-to-reschedule, multi-day event spans in month grid, side-by-side layout for overlapping events in week view, all-day event lane at the top of week view, locale-aware week start (Monday vs Sunday), and collapse-to-week on narrow widths.

items.title: Push notifications for upcoming events
items.description:
Notify attendees before an event starts. Depends on the mobile-apps push notification infrastructure (user_devices table + APNs/FCM fan-out), but also shows up as browser notifications on web.

Scope:
- Background job (cron or interval-based) that scans events with startAt in the next N minutes and dispatches notifications to RSVP'd-as-going attendees
- Configurable per-user notification lead time: 5 min / 15 min / 1 hour / day before — stored on actor.properties or a new notification_preferences entry
- Deduplication: don't send multiple notifications for the same event instance
- For recurring events, notify once per instance, not once for the parent
- Linked channel: if the event has a channelId, the notification payload includes deep-link data so tapping jumps straight to that channel (desktop: focus the channel; mobile: open the app and navigate)
- Event chat notifications: new messages in the event chat notify the RSVP'd-as-going participants (just like DM notifications)
- Respects notification_preferences (muted events, quiet hours)

Depends on: calendar-monthly-view (nice-to-have for context when the notification arrives), user_devices infrastructure from mobile-apps.

items.title: Per-instance RSVPs for recurring events
items.description:
Currently attendance lives on the parent event row, so RSVPing to a weekly meeting RSVPs to all of them (matching Google Calendar's default 'accept all' behavior). Users often want to accept the series but skip specific weeks.

Scope:
- New event_attendee_overrides table: (eventId, actorId, instanceDate, status) with a unique (eventId, actorId, instanceDate) index
- Override rows take precedence over the base RSVP when querying a specific instance
- API: POST /events/:eventId/rsvp now accepts an optional instanceDate param; omitting it updates the base, passing it creates/updates the override
- UI: EventDetailPanel shows 'This event' vs 'All events in series' toggle when opened on an instance of a recurring event
- Pair with calendar-federation: overrides federate via Join activities with a specific object URI that encodes the instance date
- Migration: existing base RSVPs remain unchanged; overrides are purely additive

Low priority until users ask for it — the current 'accept all' semantics are defensible.

items.title: Voice: second webcam/screen enable doesn't display on receiver
items.description:
After toggling webcam or screen share off then on again, the receiver doesn't see the re-enabled stream even though the sender's self-view works correctly. First enable works; second enable doesn't. See `.daedalus/open-issues/voice-video-reenable.md` for the full troubleshooting history — 11 shipped attempts across `6d94834..7c4da1b` trying different routing, reconcile, and renegotiation strategies.

Latest attempt (`7c4da1b`) removed renegotiation from the disable path — theory is that disable-time renegotiation was driving the receiver's transceiver into `readyState === 'ended'` or a stuck direction that no subsequent enable could recover from. Untested as of pause.

Next steps:
1. Test the latest commit cold tomorrow
2. If still broken, read `voice: reconcile scan` logs to see what receiver.track.readyState and muted actually report during the failing re-enable
3. Fall back to alternative strategies: `addTrack`/`removeTrack` instead of pre-allocated transceivers + replaceTrack, or PC recreation on toggle
4. Test in Firefox vs Chrome to rule out browser-specific quirks

Blocker-class for the voice channels launch story. Polish pass, group-video, and screen-share all depend on reliable enable/disable.

items.title: Server wikis with bidirectional message linking
items.description:
A per-server wiki for long-form knowledge that persists outside the chat stream. The central gap in every competitor (Discord, Slack, Teams): chat is hostile to knowledge retention. Threads scroll away, pins get buried, search finds fragments without context. A first-class wiki inside the server — with bidirectional links between wiki pages and chat messages — turns Babelr into a knowledge base that happens to have chat, not a chat app that forgets everything. Also materially differentiates us from the 'Discord clone' framing.

Architectural fit: plays directly to existing strengths — federates as ActivityPub `Article` objects (AS2 native type, no extensions needed) and runs through the tone-preserving translation pipeline we already use for messages and events. A multilingual team reading the same wiki page each in their own language is a feature no competitor can match.

Data model:
- `wiki_pages`: id, serverId, slug (unique per server), title, content (markdown), createdBy, lastEditedBy, createdAt, updatedAt, properties JSONB
- `wiki_page_revisions`: full edit history — pageId, revisionNumber, content, editedBy, editedAt, summary
- `wiki_page_links`: bidirectional link graph — sourceType ('page'|'message'), sourceId, targetType ('page'|'message'), targetId. Enables backlinks panel without N+1 scans.

Linking syntax:
- In messages: `[[Page Title]]` or `[[page-slug]]` renders as a live wiki link; auto-creates the link row for backlinks
- In wiki content: message permalinks (e.g. `babelr://server/channel/msg-id` or https URL) embed as live-updating quote blocks so wiki pages can cite specific chat moments
- 'Convert message to wiki page' action in message context menu — seeds a new page with the message content and backlinks the source message

Permissions: per-server setting — `anyone | members | mods | admins` can edit. Default `members`. Page-level overrides deferred.

UI:
- Wiki button in server sidebar alongside Calendar / Friends / Settings
- WikiPanel layout: left page tree (or recent + pinned), center content with edit/view toggle, right backlinks panel showing 'linked from' pages and messages
- Edit mode is plain markdown with a preview toggle — no WYSIWYG for PR1

Translation tie-in: wiki page content flows through the existing translation pipeline (same `useTranslation`-style hook, cache by `(pageId, targetLang, revisionNumber)`). Show-original toggle matches message UX.

PR scoping:
- **PR1** (1-2 sittings): Core CRUD. `wiki_pages` + `wiki_page_revisions` tables, server routes (list / get / create / update / delete), WikiPanel UI, sidebar integration, i18n strings. Plain markdown, no linking, no backlinks.
- **PR2** (1 sitting): `wiki_page_links` table, `[[slug]]` parser in message rendering, backlinks panel, message→page and page→message link resolution, 'convert message to page' action.
- **PR3** (1 sitting): Translation hook for wiki content, cache layer, show-original toggle.

Deferred (explicit non-goals for first ship):
- Revision history UI (the data is captured in PR1, just no diff viewer yet)
- Categories / tags
- Attachments / embedded images in wiki pages (relies on file-uploads, can reuse)
- ActivityPub `Article` federation (design is clean — outbox delivery of Article Create/Update — but deferred to a dedicated PR)
- Full-text wiki search (reuse message-search infra in a follow-up)
- Per-page permissions
- AI-suggested page creation from active discussions

Rationale for P1: materially differentiates Babelr from Discord/Slack/Teams for launch positioning; small enough to land in a few sittings; unblocks the 'Babelr as a team knowledge platform' story that the enterprise roadmap items (SSO, audit logs, retention) already assume exists.

items.title: Wiki polish pass: home page, tags, idiom glosses, register indicators, sidebar search
items.description:
Post-PR3 polish bundle for server-wikis. Four distinct capabilities worth shipping together because they all touch the WikiPanel render path and/or wiki page metadata — landing them as one PR avoids three separate rounds of touching the same component.

**Designated home page**:
- Per-server setting naming one wiki page as the 'home'. When the user opens the wiki panel for the first time in a session (or hasn't selected another page yet), that page loads instead of auto-selecting by recency.
- Server owners/mods can pick the home page from any existing page via a 'Set as home' action on the view header.
- Falls back to most-recently-updated if no home is set (current behavior).
- Storage: column on the `actors` (Group) `properties` JSONB. Single scalar, no new table needed.

**Page tags**:
- Free-form tags attached to a page. Stored as `text[]` on `wiki_pages` for simplicity — if tag filtering becomes heavy we can migrate to a normalized join table later.
- Edit UI: tag chips below the title in edit mode, comma/enter to add, click to remove.
- Sidebar: collapsible 'Tags' section listing distinct tags with counts; clicking a tag filters the page list.
- Searching wiki pages by tag feeds into the later full-text wiki search item.

**Idiom gloss surfacing on wiki pages** (new, post-PR3):
- The translation pipeline already returns `TranslationMetadata.idioms` for every chunk — explanation + equivalent — but the wiki panel currently drops that data on the floor. Messages render the `IdiomGlosses` component inline; wiki pages should do the equivalent.
- Treatment: hover/click reveals the explanation in a tooltip or marginal gloss, mirroring the message UX but scaled for long-form content. For a paragraph with multiple idioms, collapse them into a single 'N idioms flagged' indicator that expands on click.
- Validated by the mixed-language fixture (`docs/testing/wiki-mixed-language-fixture.md`) which includes two Spanish idioms (`no mordemos`, `Nadie nació sabiendo`) specifically so we can confirm they come through the pipeline.

**Per-chunk register/confidence indicators** (new, post-PR3):
- Each translated prose chunk carries register (casual/formal/sarcastic/technical/affectionate/neutral), intent, and confidence score in its metadata — all currently invisible on wiki pages.
- For long-form content, a marginal indicator per paragraph (confidence dot + register label) is more readable than a single page-level badge. Messages show one dot per message; pages should show one per chunk.
- Should respect the existing show-original toggle — indicators only render when translation is active.
- Bonus: a page-level aggregate (e.g. 'mixed registers: casual + formal + technical') could sit in the meta row next to the 'Translated from X' badge, for at-a-glance understanding of a page's overall voice.

**Sidebar page search** (new):
- Client-side filter input above the page list in the WikiPanel sidebar.
- Case-insensitive substring match on `title + slug`. No server call — filters the already-loaded summary list.
- Combines multiplicatively with the tag filter: search narrows, tag narrows further.
- Deliberately scoped to title/slug only. Full-text content search is tracked as a separate `wiki-fulltext-search` item because it needs tsvector indexing, ranking, snippets, and a results panel — basically a replay of the message-search work.

Scope all five as one PR. Estimated one-to-two sittings once core wiki is stable. The idiom-gloss and register-indicator bits are pure rendering work — no new server routes or schema changes — so they're cheap if the polish pass already has the WikiPanel open.

items.title: Additional LLM backends: OpenAI + Ollama
items.description:
Broaden the translation provider selection beyond Anthropic + browser-local Transformers.js. Three immediate wins:

1. **Choice for users who already have OpenAI keys** — a lot of people have paid GPT subscriptions and would rather reuse that spend than add a second API bill.
2. **Self-hosted / air-gapped deployments via Ollama** — the enterprise on-prem story depends on being able to run translation entirely inside the customer's network. Ollama is the cleanest path to a local LLM with decent quality, and it exposes a stable HTTP API that fits our proxy model.
3. **Model competition pressure** — tone preservation is the differentiator; being able to A/B the same prompt against Haiku vs. GPT-4o-mini vs. a local Llama lets us demonstrate the pipeline quality claim empirically rather than just asserting it.

Landed on main as 48d3559. Settings UI shows a 4-option radio with two explicit tier headers (tone-preserving vs translation-only). Shared buildPrompt/parseResponse moved into packages/shared so Ollama can use them client-side. 22 new tests added (13 parser + 9 route), full suite passing at 60/60.

items.title: Granular server permissions + roles (core system — PR1 + PR2 shipped, PR3 UI is a follow-up)
items.description:
Replace the single-string role model (`collectionItems.properties.role` = 'owner' | 'admin' | 'moderator' | 'member') with a Discord-style permission + role system. Driven by the permission audit pass — see `docs/permissions-audit.md` for the full catalog of currently gated actions.

**Data model**:
- `server_roles` (id, serverId, name, color, position, permissions jsonb string[], isDefault, isSystem, timestamps)
- `server_role_assignments` (serverId, actorId, roleId) composite PK
- `server.properties.ownerId` stays as a metadata field for display + future ownership-transfer UI, but **never consulted in permission checks**.

**Permission constants** (packages/shared/src/permissions.ts, ~19 flags):
Server: MANAGE_SERVER, MANAGE_ROLES, MANAGE_INVITES, KICK_MEMBERS, VIEW_AUDIT_LOG
Channels: VIEW_CHANNELS, MANAGE_CHANNELS
Messages: SEND_MESSAGES, MANAGE_MESSAGES, ADD_REACTIONS, ATTACH_FILES, MENTION_EVERYONE
Voice: CONNECT_VOICE, SPEAK, VIDEO
Wiki: VIEW_WIKI, CREATE_WIKI_PAGES, MANAGE_WIKI
Events: CREATE_EVENTS, MANAGE_EVENTS

Reserved-for-future: VIEW_AUDIT_LOG, MENTION_EVERYONE, SPEAK, VIDEO.

**Default roles on server creation** (all three):
- **@everyone** (isDefault, isSystem, cannot be deleted): VIEW_CHANNELS, SEND_MESSAGES, ADD_REACTIONS, ATTACH_FILES, CONNECT_VOICE, SPEAK, VIDEO, VIEW_WIKI, CREATE_WIKI_PAGES, CREATE_EVENTS.
- **Moderator**: @everyone + MANAGE_CHANNELS + MANAGE_MESSAGES + MANAGE_WIKI + MANAGE_EVENTS + MANAGE_INVITES.
- **Admin**: Moderator + MANAGE_SERVER + MANAGE_ROLES + KICK_MEMBERS.

Server creator is auto-assigned the Admin role. No bypass shortcut based on ownerId.

**Lockout protection (critical invariant)**: after any role mutation, role-assignment mutation, or member kick, the server must still have at least one member whose effective permissions include MANAGE_ROLES. Enforced inside a DB transaction: apply mutation, re-query for MANAGE_ROLES holders, rollback if count reaches zero. Returns 400 with a clear error message.

**Migration strategy**: for every existing `actors` row of type 'Group', create the three default roles and walk `collection_items` to assign existing members: `role='admin'` → Admin; `role='moderator'` → Moderator; `role='owner'` → Admin (and creator's ownerId remains in properties); `role='member'` → no assignment (@everyone is implicit). Leave the legacy `properties.role` string in place for now; PR2 stops reading it; a later chore pass drops the field.

**Helper API** (packages/server/src/permissions.ts):
- `hasPermission(db, serverId, actorId, perm): Promise<boolean>`
- `hasAllPermissions(db, serverId, actorId, perms[]): Promise<boolean>`
- `getEffectivePermissions(db, serverId, actorId): Promise<Set<string>>`

All three cache the per-(serverId, actorId) computation on `request` via a decorator so a handler that checks multiple permissions doesn't N-query the database.

**Shipping in stages**:
1. **PR1** (this session): audit doc, shared permission constants, schema + migration + default-role population, helper module, creator-as-admin on new server creation. No enforcement changes — everything backwards compatible, hardcoded role-string checks still work.
2. **PR2** (this session): replace every hardcoded `['owner','admin','moderator'].includes(role)` site with a `hasPermission` call. Fix three audit bugs: channel creation had no permission check, wiki page editing had no role check, invite listing had no role check. Run the second audit to verify every gated site is covered.
3. **PR3** (follow-up): role management UI — server settings panel 'Roles' tab with list/create/edit/delete, per-role permission checkboxes grouped by category, member panel gains a role-assignment picker per member.

**Deferred to separate items**: per-channel permission overrides (`granular-permissions-channel-overrides`), role hierarchy enforcement and ownership-transfer UI (`granular-permissions-role-hierarchy`), moderation audit log (`granular-permissions-audit-log`), per-member overrides (`granular-permissions-per-member-overrides`).

**Status**: PR1 landed on main as 6b2e87b (schema, migration, helpers, bootstrap). PR2 landed as 2000184 (enforcement pass, three audit bugs fixed, lockout invariant wired into mutation transactions, 9 new tests). PR3 landed as 24c604c (role management UI — RolesPanel with permission checkbox grid, MemberList multi-role picker, six new server endpoints, 14 new tests). End-to-end usable.

items.title: Role management UI (Roles tab, member-role assignment, role chips) — landed as 24c604c
items.description:
PR3 of the granular-permissions-core work. The data model and enforcement are on main; this adds the management surface.

Scope:
- Server settings panel gains a 'Roles' tab listing all roles for the current server (excluding @everyone by default — it shows as a special system entry at the bottom).
- Create/edit/delete role actions gated on MANAGE_ROLES. Create opens a dialog with name, color picker, and a permission checkbox grid grouped by category. Edit reuses the same dialog. Delete confirms, then fires the DELETE endpoint (new: PR3 server route).
- Per-role permission checkboxes laid out by category (Server admin, Channels, Messages, Voice, Wiki, Events), reading straight from the PERMISSIONS enum so new flags show up automatically.
- Member management panel gets a 'Roles' column per member. Click opens a multi-select dropdown of available roles; saving fires a new POST/DELETE /servers/:id/members/:id/roles/:roleId endpoint pair that inserts/deletes role-assignment rows directly (rather than going through the legacy single-role-string endpoint).
- Message author display and member list both show the member's highest-position role as a colored chip next to their name. Keeps the Discord-style 'I can see who's an admin at a glance' affordance.
- Migrates the client to read role membership from the new role-assignment tables instead of the legacy `collection_items.properties.role` string. The legacy string stays in the database for back-compat but the UI stops reading it.

New server endpoints needed (not yet in PR1/2):
- POST /servers/:serverId/roles (create role)
- PUT /servers/:serverId/roles/:roleId (edit name, color, permissions)
- DELETE /servers/:serverId/roles/:roleId (delete non-system role)
- POST /servers/:serverId/members/:actorId/roles/:roleId (assign)
- DELETE /servers/:serverId/members/:actorId/roles/:roleId (unassign)
- GET /servers/:serverId/roles (list)

All gated on MANAGE_ROLES, all wrapped in transactions that call ensureManageRolesSurvives so no mutation can empty the set of holders. Position defaults to max-existing+1 on create; reordering is a separate item (role-hierarchy follow-up).

Estimated one sitting for routes + UI once PR1/2 are merged.

Shipped as 24c604c on main. Roles tab in Server Settings, member list multi-role picker with colored chips, six new endpoints, 14 new route tests (97/97 suite). End-to-end usable.

items.title: Remaining permission enforcement: ATTACH_FILES + CONNECT_VOICE
items.description:
Two permissions exist in the enum but aren't enforced at their target handlers yet. The granular-permissions-core feature landed all the rest (15 permissions gated end-to-end and tested in permissions-matrix.test.ts); these two were deferred because neither fits cleanly into the REST route layer the current helper assumes.

**`ATTACH_FILES` — upload endpoint server-scoping**

The upload handler (`POST /upload` in `uploads.ts`) is global and authenticated but not server-scoped. Attachments get referenced later in message-send payloads. To enforce `ATTACH_FILES`, one of two approaches:

1. **Accept a `serverId` on the upload request**. The client already knows which server/channel the upload is for — pass it through and check `hasPermission(serverId, ATTACH_FILES)` before writing the file. Clean but requires a client API change.
2. **Check on message send instead**. When a message with an `attachments` property comes in, look up each attachment URL and verify the sender has `ATTACH_FILES` on the channel's server. Cheaper client-side but means uploads can be written to disk before the permission check — leaves orphaned blobs if denied.

Go with (1) — cleaner model, and the orphaned-blob concern with (2) is a real operational problem.

**`CONNECT_VOICE` — WS voice:join gating**

Voice join is a WS message, not a REST endpoint. `packages/server/src/plugins/ws.ts` handles `voice:join` by calling `fastify.voiceJoin(...)`. To enforce `CONNECT_VOICE`:

- Add a `hasPermission` check early in the `voice:join` switch arm. Easy.
- Add a WS test harness. We don't have one today — the existing tests use `app.inject(...)` for HTTP, which can't send WS frames. Options: use `@fastify/websocket`'s built-in testing hooks, or spin up a real `ws` client against the test server. The latter is more realistic but adds a dependency and some fragility.

Bundled into one PR because they're the last two gaps and both benefit from the same 'close out the enforcement story' commit. Adds a matrix test per permission to `permissions-matrix.test.ts` so the regression catch is complete for every enforced permission.

items.title: Message embed: scroll to the referenced message after navigation
items.description:
Clicking a `[[msg:slug]]` embed today switches to the source channel via `selectChannel(embed.channelId)` and calls it done. The user still has to scan or scroll to find the actual message they clicked. Fix: after the channel switches and the message list renders, scroll the referenced message into view and highlight it for a moment.

Implementation sketch:

- Extend `ChatView`'s navigation handler to set a `pendingScrollToMessageId` state alongside the channel selection.
- `MessageList` watches that state via a prop. On next render (after messages load for the new channel), it finds the matching message by id, calls `scrollIntoView({block: 'center'})` on its DOM ref, and applies a CSS class (`.highlighted` or similar) for a 2-3 second fade.
- Edge case: the target message might not be in the first page of messages. If not found, fire a `fetchMessagesAroundId(messageId)` that loads a window around the target. Reuses the existing thread-load pattern.
- Edge case: if the target channel is already selected (user clicked an embed to a message in the current channel), skip the channel-switch and go directly to scroll.
- Nice-to-have: a URL hash like `#channel/{channelId}/msg/{slug}` so the navigation is bookmarkable and browser-back works. Lower priority than the scroll itself.

One sitting of work. The feature was deliberately cut from the message-slugs landing to keep that PR bounded; picking it up independently is cheap and adds real polish.

items.title: Reverse backlinks: show 'referenced from' on messages
items.description:
The wiki panel already has a 'Linked from' section that lists pages and messages that reference the current wiki page. Message embeds introduced a symmetric case: a wiki page (or another message) can reference a specific message via `[[msg:slug]]`, but there's no surface showing those citations from the referenced message's perspective.

Why this matters: citation awareness. If Alice quotes Bob's message in a wiki page, Bob has no way to discover that happened. The one-directional link model means the reference effectively leaves the source message's context. For a knowledge graph to feel alive, the edges should be walkable from both ends.

Scope:

- **Schema**: extend `wiki_page_links` (or create a parallel `message_refs` table) to record message-as-target references. Today `wiki_page_links` has a `targetMessageId` column but the source is always a wiki page; we'd need to also track message-source → message-target refs.
- **Sync**: when a message is created/edited and its content contains `[[msg:otherSlug]]`, insert a row. When a wiki page is created/edited and its content contains `[[msg:otherSlug]]`, insert a row (may already work — verify).
- **Endpoint**: `GET /messages/:id/backlinks` returning a list of source pages + source messages that reference this message. Permission-gated the same way the lookup endpoint is.
- **UI**: MessageItem gains a small 'N references' badge when backlinks exist. Clicking expands a panel below the message showing source page titles and source message snippets, each clickable to navigate.

Scope cut: no federation for now. Backlinks are local-only, same as the message embed feature itself.

items.title: Batch message lookup endpoint for high-density embed pages
items.description:
The current `GET /messages/by-slug/:slug` endpoint works one-at-a-time. `MessageEmbed` has a module-level dedupe cache so three embeds of the *same* message share one fetch, but three embeds of *different* messages on one page still fire three serial HTTP requests. For short chat or typical wiki pages that's fine. For pages with many embeds it shows up as a visible loading cascade.

Fix: `POST /messages/by-slugs` accepting `{slugs: string[]}` and returning `{results: MessageEmbedView[]}` in a single call. Each slug individually permission-checked server-side; results for inaccessible messages omitted from the response (client treats missing slugs as 'locked').

Client change: introduce a `useMessageEmbedBatch(slugs)` hook that collects all slugs seen during a render pass and fires a single batch request, then serves the cache to each individual `MessageEmbed`. Requires a small refactor of the embed component to consume from a context-provided store rather than fetching directly on mount — nontrivial but mechanical.

Defer until actual docs hit the pain point. The module cache already handles the common case (user hovering over a message that references a popular message already shown in chat). A wiki page with 20 distinct embeds is not yet a real use case — reserve this item for when it is.

items.title: Federate message slugs across instances
items.description:
Message slugs are currently local-only. A slug generated on babelr.local uniquely identifies a message *on that server*, but `[[msg:abc1234xyz]]` in a federated context is ambiguous — which instance's message? Since ActivityPub inbound is already AP-shaped and we eventually want federation to work, this is a real gap we'll hit the moment two instances start talking.

Proposed addressing scheme: `[[msg:abc1234xyz@babelr.local]]` — slug, `@`, origin domain. Local refs (no `@`) default to the current server. Cross-instance refs fall through to a federated lookup path that hits the target instance's `/messages/by-slug/:slug` endpoint (which needs to exist and be public-read for non-private channels).

Complications:

- **Permission check on federated reads**: the target instance has to decide whether the caller can see the message. Simplest model: public channels are readable by any federated caller with proof-of-membership in some shared server; private channels return 403. Matches the AP visibility model.
- **Caching and staleness**: federated lookups should cache more aggressively than local because the round-trip is slower. Same localStorage cache we already have for translations could apply. TTL invalidation on message-edit activities.
- **Embed rendering for unreachable instances**: the target instance might be down or unreachable. Locked-message placeholder needs a distinct 'remote instance unavailable' state.

Gated on: message federation itself (currently in the AP-shaped-from-day-one bucket but not actively delivered). This item probably lands alongside whatever PR activates federated messaging for the first time — no point implementing federated slug lookup if messages aren't federated yet.

items.title: Per-channel permission overrides
items.description:
Follow-up to the granular server permissions + roles system. Discord-style per-channel permission matrix that overrides server-level role permissions for a specific channel.

Example use cases:
- An `#announcements` channel where only the Admin role has SEND_MESSAGES even though @everyone has it server-wide.
- A private #leadership channel that only specific roles can VIEW_CHANNELS for.
- A #feedback channel where @everyone loses MANAGE_MESSAGES so mods can't delete user complaints.

Scope:
- New `channel_permission_overrides` table: (channelId, roleId, permission, effect) where `effect` is 'allow' or 'deny'. Composite PK on (channelId, roleId, permission).
- Effective permission evaluation order: server-level role permissions → channel allow-overrides → channel deny-overrides → (per-member overrides if we add them later). Deny wins, allow next, base permissions last.
- Helper: `hasPermissionInChannel(db, channelId, actorId, permission)` that walks this precedence chain. Used in place of `hasPermission` for channel-scoped actions.
- UI: channel settings panel gains a 'Permissions' tab with role pickers and per-permission allow/deny/inherit toggles per role.
- Migration: no data to move. Existing channels remain fully inherited (zero override rows = fall through to server-level).

Deferred from the initial permissions PR to keep the first landing bounded. The helper API is intentionally pluggable so switching `hasPermission` to `hasPermissionInChannel` at call sites is a find-and-replace when we land this.

items.title: Role hierarchy enforcement
items.description:
Follow-up to granular server permissions + roles. Today the initial PR gates role mutations on a simple `MANAGE_ROLES` check plus a lockout-protection invariant. Discord layers one more thing on top: role hierarchy, where a user can only edit/delete roles below their highest-position role.

Scope:
- `server_roles.position` integer already exists in the initial schema — this item turns it on.
- When a user attempts to create, edit, or delete a role, the role's `position` must be strictly less than the user's highest-position role. Otherwise 403.
- When a user attempts to assign or unassign a role to another member, the target role's position must be strictly less than the caller's highest-position role.
- Kick/ban must respect hierarchy — can't kick someone whose highest-position role equals or exceeds yours.
- UI affordance: role drag-and-drop in the role management panel updates `position`. Roles above the caller's highest are shown greyed out / read-only.
- Edge case: the `@everyone` default role always has position 0 and cannot be reordered below another role (its position is the floor).

Includes adding an ownership-transfer UI flow (currently ownerId in properties is display-only; transferring means reassigning the Admin role and moving the ownerId pointer). The hierarchy rules make the transfer flow safer because the new owner has to be promoted before the old one can be demoted.

items.title: Moderation audit log
items.description:
Every moderation action on a server writes a row to an audit log table, visible to users with the `VIEW_AUDIT_LOG` permission (Admin-tier by default).

What gets logged:
- Role create/edit/delete
- Role assignment changes
- Member kicks and bans
- Channel create/edit/delete
- Message deletes by someone other than the author
- Wiki page deletes by someone other than the creator
- Server settings changes (name, description, home page, etc.)

Shape: `audit_log` table with (id, serverId, actorId, actionType, targetType, targetId, summary, beforeState jsonb, afterState jsonb, createdAt). Indexed on (serverId, createdAt desc) for fast recent-view queries.

UI: server settings panel gains an 'Audit Log' tab showing a paginated list of recent actions, filterable by action type and actor. Each entry shows 'Alice kicked Bob' or 'Carol edited the Admin role to add MANAGE_WIKI'.

The `VIEW_AUDIT_LOG` permission flag is already reserved in the initial permissions enum so this is purely additive — add the table, add the writes at each mutation site, add the read endpoint and UI tab. No other code changes.

items.title: Per-member permission overrides (rare-but-sometimes-needed)
items.description:
The initial permission system is purely role-based. Discord supports per-member overrides on top of that — granting or denying a specific permission to a specific user regardless of their roles. Use cases are rare but real: 'this one user can post in #announcements even though they're not in the Admin role', or 'this one user is temporarily muted server-wide'.

This is explicitly low priority because role-based works for ~95% of cases and per-member state tends to rot (users leave, grants accumulate, no one remembers why). Only worth shipping if we hit a concrete user need that can't be solved by making a one-person role.

If we do ship it:
- New `server_member_permission_overrides` (serverId, actorId, permission, effect) table.
- Permission evaluation: role permissions → channel overrides → member overrides. Member overrides win all ties.
- UI: a per-member 'override' section in the member management panel. Discouraged by default — the UI should recommend 'create a role' instead when the user is about to grant the third override to the same actor.

items.title: Full-text wiki page content search
items.description:
The wiki polish pass ships a client-side title/slug filter in the sidebar. That handles 'I know the page name' but not 'I remember a phrase from inside a page', which is the search case that actually matters when the wiki has grown past a handful of pages.

Scope:
- Add a tsvector column to `wiki_pages` (mirroring the `objects.content_search` column used for message search). Populate on write via a trigger or in the server route.
- New route `GET /servers/:serverId/wiki/search?q=...` that does a ts_rank-ordered match against the tsvector. Return `WikiSearchHit[]` — page summary plus a ts_headline snippet with highlighted matches.
- Client: when the sidebar search input has more than N characters, fall through from the client-side title filter into a debounced server call; render results as a distinct 'content matches' section below the 'title matches' list.
- Reuses the infrastructure from the existing message-search feature — indexing, tsvector helpers, Postgres stopwords config, highlighting pattern.
- Permission-aware: only return hits from pages the caller can access (same membership check as list/get).

One sitting once the polish pass has landed. The ship order matters: the polish pass changes the sidebar layout that this feature will slot into.

items.title: Migrate chat message translation cache onto hashed localStorage layer
items.description:
PR3 of server-wikis landed a localStorage-backed translation cache with two APIs: a hashed `(kind, contentHash, targetLang)` layer used by wiki, and a legacy `(messageId, targetLang)` layer used by chat messages. The legacy layer was kept intact to keep the wiki PR from ballooning, but leaves three issues on the chat side:

1. **No edit invalidation**. Messages can be edited (the edit-history feature is done). If a user edits a message, its cache entry still points at the pre-edit translation until the legacy layer is rewritten to invalidate on content change. Today this is masked because edits tombstone the old message row, but the cached translation still lives in localStorage under the same key.

2. **No cross-message dedupe**. Two users sending identical content in two different channels each get their own cache entry. Hashing would collapse those into one — meaningful for common phrases like greetings, reactions-as-messages, and copy-pasted error strings.

3. **Two parallel APIs in one file**. `cache.ts` is carrying both shapes; over time the drift cost accumulates. Messages should be on the same hashed layer as wiki and (eventually) DMs so there's exactly one cache API to reason about.

**Migration plan**:
- Switch `useTranslation` to call `getCachedByHash('message', hashContent(msg.content), lang)` / `setCachedByHash(...)` instead of `getCached(id, lang)` / `setCached(...)`.
- Delete the legacy `getCached`/`setCached`/`legacyKey` code path and the `babelr:tx:message-id:` prefix — there's no migration path needed because translations are auto-refillable on next fetch, so worst case a user's first session after the update does a one-time re-translation of whatever's still in their chat history.
- Keep the `ContentKind` enum as-is — this just starts using the `'message'` value it already has.
- Message-level invalidation (edits, tombstones) becomes automatic because the hash changes with the content.
- No schema changes. No server changes. Single-file edit on the client plus the cache.ts cleanup.

**Optional stretch**: same migration for DMs (`ContentKind = 'dm'`). Same shape of change, same file region. Probably worth bundling because it's the last consumer of the legacy API.

Estimated one short sitting. Worth doing before messages pick up any other cache-adjacent feature so we're not locking in the dual-API problem any further.

items.title: Desktop voice on Linux — webkit2gtk WebRTC availability
items.description:
Tauri on Linux uses wry → webkit2gtk as the embedded webview, and several major distributions (Arch, and historically others) ship webkit2gtk compiled WITHOUT WebRTC support. This is verifiable by grepping the library for RTCPeerConnection implementation symbols: `createOffer`, `createAnswer`, `setLocalDescription`, and `addIceCandidate` are absent even though `webkit_settings_set_enable_webrtc` exists (it's just a property setter on a stubbed-out feature). Voice channels fail to join in the desktop app on these distros with 'Can't find variable RTCPeerConnection'.

The client (`useVoice.join`) now detects this case and surfaces a clear error directing users to open Babelr in Firefox or Chromium to join voice channels from the same machine. Text, DMs, events, friends, translation, and everything else continues to work in the desktop app.

Mitigation options to revisit:
- Monitor Arch's webkit2gtk package for a WebRTC-enabled build (community pressure exists)
- Build a distribution-specific AppImage bundling a webkit2gtk with WebRTC enabled
- Switch to Chromium Embedded Framework (CEF) or a custom Electron-style embed for Linux — large undertaking, breaks consistency with Tauri's story
- Implement WebRTC natively in Rust (via webrtc-rs) and expose it to the webview via a JS bridge — complex but gives us full control and works around the webkit gap

The Rust-side settings flip (set_enable_webrtc, set_enable_media_stream, set_enable_mediasource, auto-allow permission requests) is still in place because it's the correct configuration for distros that DO have WebRTC (Fedora, Debian, Ubuntu). This item tracks the Arch-specific gap as a known limitation, not a bug in Babelr.

items.title: Translation of event titles and descriptions
items.description:
Run event titles and descriptions through the same tone-preserving translation pipeline we use for messages, so a reader sees the event details in their preferredLanguage while the original is preserved.

Shipped:
- New useEventTranslation hook mirroring the useWikiTranslation pattern. Takes an EventView[] and translates title + description per event via the existing `/translate` endpoint.
- Content-hash cache keys (separate for title and description) via the shared hashed cache API with ContentKind='event', so two events with the same title share a cache hit and edits auto-invalidate.
- Translated titles render in the EventsPanel list rows — no explicit indicator, list stays clean.
- EventDetailPanel renders translated title + description with a show-original toggle next to the meta row and a 'Translating event details…' loading state. Matches the existing message and wiki translation UX.
- 'events.translating', 'events.showOriginal', 'events.showTranslation' i18n strings added with {lang} interpolation.

Delivered as a single PR after the message-embed landing. End-to-end usable for the multilingual team scheduling case described in the original scope.

items.title: Voice channels — P2P WebRTC, same-instance, ≤8 participants
items.description: Shipped in 428ea6a (core) and 8f78c24 (polish pass: deafen, push-to-talk, per-peer volume, speaking indicator). P2P WebRTC mesh, same-instance, 8-participant cap. Mute / deafen / PTT / per-peer volume / speaking indicator all working. Follow-ups tracked as separate items: voice-channels-video (webcam), screen-sharing, voice-channels-sfu (>8 peers), voice-channels-federation, voice-realtime-translation. Linux desktop caveat captured in desktop-voice-linux-webkit-gap.

items.title: Voice channels SFU — scale beyond 8 participants
items.description: After voice-channels-mvp ships, add an SFU (Selective Forwarding Unit) so voice channels can scale to dozens of participants. Options: mediasoup (Node, embedded), LiveKit (separate Go service), or roll our own. Adds operational burden — media ports, TURN servers, observability stack.

items.title: Federated voice channels
items.description: Cross-instance voice. Requires inventing a signaling protocol on top of ActivityPub (or a sidecar) since AP carries no SDP/ICE. No federated chat app currently does this. Research project, not a near-term ship.

items.title: Enterprise SSO — SAML + OIDC
items.description:
Single most important enterprise gate. No business will roll out a chat app where employees create individual `babelr` passwords; they need to integrate with Okta, Azure AD, Google Workspace, or any standards-compliant IdP.

Scope:
- SAML 2.0 service provider implementation (assertion verification, signing, encryption support, metadata exchange)
- OIDC client implementation (authorization code flow, JWT validation, refresh tokens)
- Per-server SSO config: each server admin can wire their own IdP, so a federated/multi-tenant deploy can have multiple orgs with different IdPs against one Babelr instance
- Just-in-time user provisioning on first login (create the actor row from SAML/OIDC claims)
- Admin UI: SP metadata download, IdP metadata upload/URL, attribute mapping for displayName/email/groups
- Group claim → server role mapping (so IdP groups can drive admin/mod assignment)
- Fallback to password auth controlled per-server (force-SSO-only mode for regulated tenants)
- Session management: SSO logout (SLO) propagation

Procurement teams will literally not approve a chat tool without this. P0 enterprise blocker.

items.title: Admin audit logs + queryable history
items.description:
Every admin action, every login, every channel deletion, every role change, every message edit/delete with timestamps and actor IDs, queryable and exportable. SOC2 audits will literally ask for this.

Scope:
- New `audit_log` table: id, timestamp, actorId, actorType (user|system|federation), action, targetType, targetId, beforeJson, afterJson, ipAddress, userAgent, properties
- Hooked into every mutation path: auth (login/logout/registration/password change), channel CRUD, server CRUD, role assignments, kicks, message edit/delete, file uploads, federation deliveries, friend actions, calendar/event CRUD
- Admin UI: filterable log viewer (by actor, action type, date range, target), CSV export
- Retention: indefinite by default; tied into the data-retention-policies feature once that lands
- Tamper-evidence: append-only design, periodic hash chain over rows for SOC2-friendly integrity proof
- Federation: outbound deliveries logged; inbound activities logged with verification status

Pairs naturally with eDiscovery export — both read from the same log tables.

items.title: Configurable data retention + legal hold + eDiscovery export
items.description:
Admin-configurable message retention. Often regulated: FINRA wants 7 years, HIPAA wants 6, GDPR wants the opposite (delete on request). Companies will not deploy without the ability to comply with whichever regime applies to them.

Scope:
- Per-server retention policy: 'delete messages older than N days' OR 'keep everything forever'. Configurable in Server Settings.
- Per-channel override: a #compliance channel can have a longer retention window than #random.
- Background job: periodically scans messages older than the policy and tombstones them (preserving the existing federation Delete activity path).
- Legal hold: admin can flag a user, channel, or date range as exempt from retention deletion. Hold rows are append-only and can only be released by an audit-logged action.
- eDiscovery export: admin can export all messages from a user / channel / date range as a structured archive (JSON + attachments) for legal review. Export should be tamper-evident (hash manifest, signed metadata).
- Per-user GDPR export: a user can request all their data, get a downloadable archive, exercise right-to-erasure (where not blocked by legal hold).
- All retention/hold/export actions write to audit-logs.

Depends on `audit-logs` for the hooks.

items.title: Webcam video in voice channels
items.description:
Add optional per-participant camera streams to voice channels. Discord-style model: a voice channel is the 'room' and each participant has three independent streams — audio (on when joined), webcam video (optional toggle), screen share (optional toggle). Not a separate 'video call' feature.

Scope:
- 'Turn on camera' button in VoicePanel that calls getUserMedia({ video: true, audio: false }) and addTrack()s the video track to every existing RTCPeerConnection
- Per-peer RTCRtpSender.replaceTrack when toggling, NOT renegotiation — avoids an offer/answer round trip for a local state change
- Client renders remote video tracks in a grid layout (replacing or augmenting the current avatar-only tiles) with each peer's tile showing their video when active, fallback to avatar when off
- Local self-view preview
- Handle track ended events (user disabled via OS/browser controls)
- Bandwidth considerations: at 8 peers × 720p each, mesh topology gets heavy fast; document practical limit of ~4 peers with video, gate the rest behind the SFU roadmap item
- Mobile-friendly fallback: on narrow viewports, grid collapses to a single large tile with swipeable peer selection

## Shared infrastructure with screen-sharing

This feature and `screen-sharing` share about 60% of implementation:
- Both add a video track to existing peer connections via addTrack/replaceTrack
- Both need an ontrack handler extension for kind='video'
- Both need receiving-side <video> element rendering
- Both need cleanup on track.ended and peer-left

The differences are:
- Acquisition: getUserMedia({ video }) vs getDisplayMedia()
- UX: grid of equal video tiles (webcam) vs one focused big view + small peer strip (screen share)
- Toggle UI: camera-on/off button vs share-screen button

Recommended implementation order: ship whichever is needed first, then the second gets ~40% additional work. Building them in the same sitting is ideal — the shared video-track machinery only needs to be written once.

items.title: Screen sharing in voice channels
items.description:
Standard for any business meeting tool. WebRTC's `getDisplayMedia` is one extra track on the existing peer connection — simple to implement once voice-channels-mvp ships.

Scope:
- 'Share screen' button in the voice panel UI, prompts native browser screen picker
- New media track added to existing RTCPeerConnections (no new signaling shape needed beyond existing voice signaling)
- Receiver renders the screen track in a larger video element distinct from the voice avatars
- Only one participant sharing at a time in the MVP (lock + handoff)
- Audio capture from the shared tab/window optional via getDisplayMedia constraints
- Works on Chrome, Firefox, Edge, Safari (with Safari quirks for window-only sharing)

## Shared infrastructure with voice-channels-video

This feature and `voice-channels-video` share about 60% of implementation — see the webcam item's description for the split. Building them in the same sitting is ideal; the shared video-track machinery only needs to be written once. If shipping them separately, do webcam first since it's the higher-visibility feature (enterprise video call parity), then screen share becomes a ~1-sitting add-on.

items.title: SCIM 2.0 user lifecycle provisioning
items.description:
Auto-create users when HR adds them, deactivate when HR removes them. Pairs with enterprise-sso — both are part of the standard enterprise identity stack.

Scope:
- SCIM 2.0 service provider endpoints (RFC 7644): /Users, /Groups, /ServiceProviderConfig, /ResourceTypes, /Schemas
- Bearer token auth for SCIM, configured per-server alongside SSO
- Filter / pagination support per the spec (Okta and Azure AD are picky about this)
- Group sync: SCIM groups map to server roles
- Soft-delete on deprovisioning (preserve message attribution; mark account inactive)
- Idempotent — provisioning agents will retry; design for it

Entries in audit-logs for every provisioning action.

items.title: Guest accounts with scoped channel access
items.description:
Invite a contractor or external client into one channel without giving them the rest of the server. Currently private channels exist but there's no proper 'external user' concept with limited scope — joining a server still grants visibility into the server's existence and any other channels they get added to.

Scope:
- New actor type or role: 'guest', flagged on the actor row
- Guests can only see channels they're explicitly added to — server discovery, member list, other channels are hidden
- Server admin invites a guest by email or handle; guest accepts via invite link, no full registration required for cross-instance guests
- Guest sessions can be time-limited (auto-expire after N days)
- Visual badge in member list and message author display so internal users can tell who's external
- Audit-logged guest actions distinct from member actions

items.title: Slack/Teams workspace import tool
items.description:
Migration tooling. Companies switching chat platforms will not manually rebuild years of channels, members, and message history. Even a basic workspace import gets us in the door for bake-off evaluations.

Scope:
- Slack export ZIP parser: channels, channel members, messages, threads, attachments, custom emoji
- Microsoft Teams export parser (via MS Graph or Teams Export API)
- Map Slack/Teams users to Babelr accounts (by email if SSO, otherwise admin-driven mapping)
- Preserve threading, reactions, attachments, timestamps
- Run as a background job with progress reporting in admin UI
- Idempotent: re-importing the same export updates rather than duplicates
- Federation-aware: imported messages get local URIs and don't get re-broadcast as new

High-leverage for sales but heavy implementation. Probably P2 backlog unless a specific deal requires it.

items.title: Group end-to-end encryption via MLS
items.description:
Currently only DMs are E2E (pairwise ECDH + AES-GCM). Channels are server-readable. Enterprise wants the option to flip channels into encrypted mode for sensitive discussions where the home server is not trusted with plaintext.

The existing pairwise ECDH doesn't generalize to groups — you'd need O(n²) sessions and rekey on every membership change. The right answer is MLS (Messaging Layer Security, RFC 9420), which is the IETF standard for group E2E and is what Signal, WhatsApp, Wire, and Element are converging on.

Scope:
- MLS implementation: tree-based key agreement, group state, welcome messages, commit/proposal flow, forward secrecy, post-compromise security
- Library option: openmls (Rust, has WASM bindings), or eventlymls
- Per-channel opt-in: server admin enables encryption on a channel; existing messages stay plaintext, new messages are MLS-encrypted
- Group state synced via existing federation delivery for cross-instance encrypted channels
- Mobile/desktop UI for verifying safety numbers and managing trust
- Key backup / recovery for users who lose devices (escrow with passphrase, or device sync)
- Significant testing burden — encryption bugs are silent and catastrophic

Multi-week project. The kind of feature that alone would justify a security audit before launch.

items.title: Self-hosted on-prem package + license model
items.description:
Many regulated industries (healthcare, finance, defense, government) will not put chat in any vendor's SaaS at any price. They need to deploy inside their own firewall.

Scope:
- Single-binary or single-container deployment with PostgreSQL bundled or pluggable
- Air-gap support: nothing phones home, no telemetry, optional Anthropic translation but works without any external API calls if local translation is configured
- License key system if commercial — gate enterprise features (SSO, audit logs, retention) behind a license check
- Documented installation guide for common platforms (Docker Compose, Kubernetes Helm chart, bare-metal systemd)
- Update mechanism that doesn't require internet access
- Backup/restore CLI for snapshotting database + uploads
- Hardening guide: TLS termination, reverse proxy, firewall rules, log aggregation hooks

This is also where the federation story becomes a real selling point — 'self-host inside your firewall and still federate with partner companies who have their own deployment' is something Slack and Teams literally cannot offer.

items.title: Trust signal layer — SOC2, status page, security policy, DPA
items.description:
Not engineering work per se, but real launch tasks that gate enterprise adoption. Procurement teams and security reviewers ask for these by name.

Checklist:
- Public security disclosures policy + responsible reporting address (security@)
- SOC2 Type 1 audit (months of process work — policies, controls, evidence collection — typically via Vanta or Drata)
- SOC2 Type 2 follow-up (6+ months of operating evidence after Type 1)
- Public status page (statuspage.io, BetterStack, or self-hosted) with incident history
- Documented uptime SLA for hosted offering
- DPA (Data Processing Agreement) template for GDPR / CCPA customers
- Sub-processor list (Anthropic, hosting provider, etc.) published
- Incident response plan documented
- Penetration test from a reputable firm before broad enterprise rollout
- Dependency vulnerability scanning in CI (Snyk, Dependabot, etc.)

Mostly process and paperwork, but a hard gate at procurement. Track separately from feature work.

items.title: Pluggable [[kind:slug]] embed system — validated by project-management plugin
items.description:
The current [[msg:slug]] / [[event:slug]] / [[page-slug]] embeds are hardcoded kinds in the shared wiki-links parser, with dedicated React components (MessageEmbed, EventEmbed) rendered by renderWithEmbeds. This is great for first-party kinds but caps at whatever we ship ourselves. A plugin system would let third parties add new ref kinds that render real content inside Babelr.

Motivating example: an Azure DevOps plugin that registers the 'ado' kind, so a [[ado:pr-1234]] in a wiki page or chat message renders the actual pull request — title, author, status, file list, diff viewer, comments — without leaving Babelr. Same pattern for Jira tickets ([[jira:PROJ-456]]), GitHub PRs ([[gh:owner/repo#123]]), Linear issues, Figma frames, Notion pages, Google Docs, and so on.

Scope:

Plugin manifest
- Each plugin declares: kind string (must not collide with built-ins), display name, icon, whether it needs backend credentials, client entry point, server entry point (optional)
- Kind registration happens at server startup; conflict detection prevents two plugins from claiming the same prefix
- Manifests loaded from a plugins directory or an npm-package convention

Parser extension
- wiki-links.ts parseWikiRefs gains a registered-kinds argument (or a registry lookup) so unknown prefixes fall through to page refs as today but recognized prefixes become typed refs
- Parser stays pure and synchronous — all the dynamic behavior lives in the resolver layer above it

Client-side plugin API
- Plugins export a React component with a stable prop shape: { slug, onNavigate } (same contract as MessageEmbed/EventEmbed)
- A plugin registry maps kind → lazy-loaded component; renderWithEmbeds consults the registry and falls back to a generic 'unknown kind' placeholder for unregistered prefixes
- Plugins may ship their own styles scoped under .embed-<kind> to avoid global collisions
- Safe-mode sandboxing: plugin components render inside an iframe or a React error boundary with strict CSP, so a broken plugin can't crash the host page

Server-side plugin API
- Plugins may register Fastify routes under /plugins/<kind>/* for their backing data fetches
- Credentials storage: per-user or per-server OAuth tokens for the plugin's upstream service, stored encrypted and fetched via a plugin-scoped secrets API
- Rate limiting and audit logging applied at the plugin boundary so a misbehaving plugin can't DoS the server or leak credentials
- Optional federation passthrough: if a plugin chooses to, it can publish resolved content into the AP graph so remote instances without the plugin still see a snapshot

Permissions + trust
- Server admins opt in to plugins — plugins aren't active just because they exist on disk
- Per-plugin permission grants (e.g. 'this Azure DevOps plugin may read PRs but not write comments')
- User-facing indicator that a plugin is the source of the content, with a click-through to the plugin's homepage/source
- Sandbox review model: first-party plugins live in packages/plugins/, third-party plugins require explicit install and show a trust warning on first use

Developer experience
- @babelr/plugin-sdk package exporting the types and helpers plugins import
- Example plugin in the monorepo (likely the ADO one, since it's the motivating case)
- Docs walking through manifest, client component, server route, credential storage, testing
- Plugin scaffolder CLI: npx @babelr/create-plugin my-plugin

Open questions
- Do we allow plugins to contribute toolbar buttons / slash commands, or is this strictly a rendering extension? (recommend starting rendering-only, expand later)
- How do plugins handle translation of their content? Probably surface a translate() hook the host can call, with the plugin deciding whether the content is worth translating at all
- What's the update story — do plugins auto-update, or do admins pin versions?

This is a meaty feature but it's what turns Babelr from a chat+wiki tool into an extensible workspace. The embed substrate is already in place; extending it to third parties is mostly API design and security hardening rather than net-new infrastructure.

--- IMPLEMENTATION NOTE ---

The project-management roadmap item is now the reference implementation for this plugin system. PM will be built entirely through the plugin API in packages/plugins/project-management/, proving the API is expressive enough for a real workload (kanban boards, work item CRUD, sprint planning, comment threads, translation hooks, federation) before third parties try to use it.

Shipping order: plugin infrastructure first, then PM as the first plugin, then the ADO/Jira/Linear plugins that motivated the original design. The PM plugin IS the documentation — third-party developers read it as a real-world example instead of a toy hello-world plugin.

Calendars, wikis, and files remain hardcoded first-party surfaces. PM is the boundary where the plugin model starts, because it is the first specialized surface that not every instance needs.

items.title: Wiki content import from common sources
items.description:
Enterprises considering Babelr as a replacement for Confluence, Notion, SharePoint, or similar already have years of accumulated wiki content they can't leave behind. A greenfield deployment with an empty wiki is a non-starter for those buyers. A first-class import workflow removes that blocker and turns 'we'd need to migrate everything manually' into 'point us at the export and we'll handle it.'

Scope:

Source formats (tiered by strategic value)

Tier 1 — highest enterprise value:
- Confluence (Cloud + Server/Data Center) — XML space export, attachment archive, page hierarchy, storage-format macros. The big one for Atlassian migration stories. Confluence storage format is XHTML with custom macros that need translation.
- Notion — Markdown+CSV bulk export (official), preserves page tree, databases become CSV, embedded media sits next to the markdown files. Fairly clean source format.
- SharePoint / OneNote — trickier, proprietary formats. Likely via a Graph API pull with OAuth rather than a file upload. Deferred until there's real demand.

Tier 2 — common in dev-centric orgs:
- MediaWiki XML export (Special:Export) — standard dump format used by Wikipedia and most self-hosted wikis. Wikitext markup requires a converter.
- GitHub / GitLab wikis — just git repositories of Markdown or other markup; clone and walk the tree.
- Obsidian vaults — folder of Markdown files with [[wikilinks]] that map very cleanly to our own syntax. Probably the cheapest import to implement because the format is closest to ours.
- DokuWiki — flat-file PHP wiki, distinctive markup syntax, popular in older enterprise deployments.

Tier 3 — nice to have:
- Google Docs / Google Sites — via Drive export, probably DOCX or HTML
- Microsoft Word / DOCX / HTML dumps — lowest-common-denominator fallback for orgs whose 'wiki' is actually a shared drive of documents
- Bookstack, Wiki.js, TiddlyWiki, Outline — smaller but vocal communities

Core workflow
- Admin navigates to Server Settings → Wiki → Import
- Picks a source format from a dropdown, uploads the archive (or authorizes an OAuth connection for live-pull sources)
- Server extracts and parses the archive, streams progress updates via WebSocket
- Preview step shows a tree of pages-to-be-created with counts, detected attachments, link resolution stats, any parse errors
- Admin confirms; import runs as a background job and creates pages, attachments, tags, and tries to rewire internal links into [[slug]] refs
- On completion, admin sees a report: N pages imported, M attachments uploaded, K broken links, any skipped content with reasons

Content conversion pipeline
- Per-format parser converts source markup to our internal markdown + extensions (e.g. the Obsidian [[wikilinks]] parser is trivial; the Confluence storage-format parser is a months-long project)
- Attachments uploaded through the existing file upload pipeline, URLs rewritten to point at the new canonical paths
- Internal wiki links resolved: if the import contains 'Engineering Onboarding' and another page links to it, the link becomes [[engineering-onboarding]] using our slug convention
- External links preserved as-is
- Tables, code blocks, images, lists, headings round-trip faithfully; everything else is best-effort with a warning in the report
- Optional translation pass: if the target server has a preferred language, run imported content through the existing wiki translation pipeline so imported docs arrive already translated

Permissions + scoping
- Import is a server-admin-only action, gated on a new MANAGE_WIKI_IMPORT permission
- Imported pages inherit the server's default wiki page permissions; admin can optionally scope the import to a specific wiki section
- Dry-run mode that validates the archive and produces a report without actually writing any pages

Idempotency + rollback
- Each import run gets an import_id tracked in a new wiki_imports table; imported pages carry that id as a foreign key
- 'Undo last import' deletes all pages created by that import_id, giving admins a safety net if the preview didn't catch problems
- Re-running the same import is either idempotent (pages updated in place by stable slug) or rejected (if the admin wants to start fresh), selected at run time

Federation consideration
- Imported pages should generate the same Create activities as hand-written pages so remote followers see them arrive. Optional batch mode suppresses the notification flood for very large imports.

Developer experience
- Importers live in packages/server/src/wiki-import/<format>/ with a consistent Importer interface: parse → preview → apply → report
- Adding a new format is purely additive — the UI dropdown picks up new importers automatically
- Shared helpers for slug generation, link resolution, attachment upload, and the progress reporter

Shipping order
1. Obsidian importer (cheapest, tests the substrate)
2. MediaWiki XML (standard format, unlocks many self-hosted wikis)
3. Confluence XML (biggest enterprise payoff, also the biggest parsing project)
4. Notion Markdown+CSV (rounds out the Tier 1 targets)
5. GitHub/GitLab wiki repos (trivial once the core substrate exists)
6. Defer SharePoint / Google / DOCX until there's a specific customer asking

Open questions
- Do we store the original source content alongside the converted page for future re-conversion if the importer improves? (probably yes, in a wiki_import_artifacts table)
- How do we handle conflicts if an importer would overwrite a hand-edited page? (prompt per-page, or global policy: skip / overwrite / rename)
- Confluence storage-format macros are the hardest part — do we try to convert common ones (table of contents, info/warning panels, task lists) or just drop them with a warning? Probably the former for the top 10 macros, the latter beyond that.

This is a clear enterprise-sales lever: the moment a prospect says 'but we have 8 years of Confluence' the answer is 'upload your space export and give us 20 minutes' instead of 'you'll need to rewrite everything by hand.'

items.title: File attachments + inline image rendering on wiki pages (superseded by wiki-attachments-via-files)
items.description: Superseded by wiki-attachments-via-files. The original scope (separate upload pipeline, per-page storage, orphan cleanup) was eliminated by the server-files feature — wiki attachments are now just file picker inserts of [[file:slug]] refs into the shared file library.

items.title: Clean up dev DB schema drift from the migration repair
items.description:
After the migration history was consolidated into a single 0004_baseline_consolidated.sql, the dev DB's schema still carries the artifacts of the broken incremental apply path:

- Duplicate foreign-key constraints on events and event_attendees tables (e.g. both `event_attendees_actor_id_fk` AND `event_attendees_event_id_events_id_fk` exist — one with drizzle's standard naming, one with the hand-written name from the original broken migration)
- Legacy constraint names on several other tables: `events_uri_key`, `invites_code_key`, `invites_created_by_fkey`, `invites_server_id_fkey`, `notification_preferences_actor_id_fkey` — should all follow drizzle's standardized naming (`_unique`, `_<target>_id_fk`)
- Possibly other column-order or naming drift that the pg_dump comparison surfaced as cosmetic

None of this affects correctness — the app works, all constraints enforce the same rules, and the dev DB is marked as up-to-date in `drizzle.__drizzle_migrations`. But a fresh DB created from the current baseline is cleaner than the dev DB, so the two are not bit-identical.

The cleanest fix is to drop and recreate the dev DB from scratch using the current baseline migration, then re-seed any test data you care about. The nuisance is obviously the loss of any ad-hoc data you've accumulated (test servers, test events, test wiki pages, etc).
