Alternative: write a one-off SQL script that drops the legacy-named constraints and the duplicates, leaving only the drizzle-standard ones. That preserves dev data but is more manual work and needs careful testing.

Not urgent — leave the drift in place until either (a) you hit a specific annoyance that makes it worth the dev data loss, or (b) you're about to onboard a contributor and want their fresh checkout to match yours exactly.

See /tmp/migration-repair/schema.diff (or re-run pg_dump --schema-only against both DBs) for the full list of remaining differences if you do decide to fix it.

items.title: Federate actor profile updates (display name, avatar, bio)
items.description:
When a user changes their display name, avatar, or bio on one instance, remote instances that have them as a friend (or have otherwise cached them) continue to show the stale values until the 24-hour resolver cache expires. This is one of the first things any real user will notice — federating a friend request but not the display name makes the whole federation story look broken.

Fix:
- On POST /auth/profile (and any other route that mutates actor.displayName, summary, or properties.avatarUrl), enqueue an Update(Actor) activity via the delivery queue to every remote inbox that has a relationship with this actor. Start with the friends set (accepted friendships row with a remote otherActorId) — that's the smallest-possible audience that covers the visible-papercut case.
- Extend federation/inbox.ts handleUpdate to detect when the inner object's `type` is `Person` or `Group` and write the new fields back to the cached `actors` row (displayName, summary, properties.avatarUrl), instead of only handling Note updates.
- Consider tightening the 24-hour STALE_THRESHOLD_MS in resolve.ts for actors we have an active relationship with. For friends, 24h is too long; for one-off message-chain actors it's fine.

Scope-adjacent follow-ups to consider as separate items:
- Group actor metadata updates (server name/icon/description changing on origin → remote followers see the change). Same Update(Actor) shape, different audience (group followers instead of friends).
- Actor deletion — when someone deletes their account, deliver Delete(Actor) to remote peers and have them remove or tombstone the cached row.

items.title: Federate channel lifecycle events (create, rename, topic, delete)
items.description:
After join-remote, the remote instance caches a snapshot of the server's channels as local shadow objects. But new channels created on the origin after the join don't appear on the remote until someone manually re-joins or a refresh mechanism fires. Similarly, channel renames, topic changes, and deletions on the origin don't propagate to the shadow objects.

Fix: when the origin creates/updates/deletes a channel, broadcast a Create/Update/Delete(OrderedCollection) activity to the Group's remote followers. The receiving instance creates/updates/tombstones the shadow object accordingly. Also add a periodic or on-select refresh that re-fetches GET /groups/:slug/channels to catch any activities that were missed (e.g. during downtime).

Small but visible gap — a user on the remote instance clicks a server and sees a stale channel list. Worth fixing before any public federation launch.

items.title: Route federated message edits and deletes through channel context resolution
items.description:
The channel discovery work added context-URI resolution to handleCreate so inbound Notes land in the correct shadow channel. But handleUpdate and handleDelete still operate on Notes by URI without resolving the channel context — so a federated message edit or delete doesn't trigger a WS broadcast to the shadow channel's subscribers, meaning remote viewers don't see edits or deletions in real time.

Fix: after updating/tombstoning the Note in handleUpdate/handleDelete, look up the stored Note's context (shadow channel id), and broadcast a message:updated or message:deleted WS event to that channel's subscribers. The outbound Update/Delete activities already carry the Note URI, so the receiver can find the stored Note and its context. Small change, high visibility — messages appearing to be uneditable/undeletable from the remote side is a confusing UX.

items.title: Cross-instance voice channels via TURN relay
items.description:
Voice channels use P2P WebRTC mesh between participants on the same instance. Cross-instance voice doesn't work because the signaling (offer/answer/ICE) is relayed through the local WS and there's no mechanism for a remote participant's browser to exchange SDP with peers on the origin instance.

Two paths:
1. Forward voice signaling activities through the federation delivery queue so the remote user's browser can establish peer connections with participants on the origin. Requires a shared TURN server that both instances can reach for NAT traversal.
2. SFU approach: the origin runs a media relay (e.g. mediasoup/Janus) and remote participants connect to it directly via WebRTC. More infrastructure but better quality at scale.

Either way this is a significant project — voice federation is a hard problem that even mature platforms (Matrix/Element) have only recently solved. Defer until the basic federation story is stable and there's real demand for cross-instance voice.

items.title: Replace unauthenticated by-slug proxy with HTTP-signed federation requests
items.description:
The message embed proxy (GET /messages/by-slug/:slug) currently allows unauthenticated access for public-channel messages so remote instances can resolve [[msg:slug]] embeds without a session cookie. This works but is too permissive for enterprise deployments that want to lock down their instance — any crawler or unauthorized client can enumerate public message slugs and read content.

The right fix: use HTTP Signatures for the proxy request, same as inbox deliveries. When instance B needs to resolve a slug on instance A, B signs the GET request with the requesting user's (or the instance's) actor key. A verifies the signature, checks that the signing actor is a member of the server that owns the channel, and returns the embed data. Unauthorized or non-member requests get 404 as before.

This requires:
- A signed-GET helper in the federation layer (currently only POST deliveries are signed)
- The by-slug endpoint to accept either session auth OR a valid HTTP Signature from a known actor
- The proxy code in the by-slug fallback to use the signed-GET helper instead of a plain fetch

Small scope, but touches the signature infrastructure which needs careful testing. The current unauthenticated path should be removed once this lands — it's a temporary expedient, not a design choice.

Also worth auditing: the /groups/:slug/channels and /groups/:slug/members endpoints are similarly unauthenticated. Those should also move to signed requests for consistency, though they're less sensitive (channel names and member lists vs message content).

items.title: Federate emoji reactions across instances
items.description:
Reactions (Like activities in AP terms) don't cross instance boundaries. When alice reacts to bob's message on A, bob doesn't see the reaction on B, and vice versa.

Outbound: when a user adds a reaction, deliver a Like(Note) activity to the Group's inbox (for server channels) or to the remote participant's inbox (for DMs). When removed, deliver an Undo(Like). The activity should carry the emoji and the Note URI so the receiver can match it.

Inbound: new handleLike / handleUndoLike in inbox.ts that finds the local Note by URI, upserts/deletes the reaction row, and broadcasts a reaction:add/reaction:remove WS event to the channel's subscribers.

Small scope — reactions are simple single-field objects. Main risk is the N reactions × M followers fanout volume on a busy channel, but the delivery queue handles batching already.

items.title: Federate server metadata updates (name, icon, description)
items.description:
When the origin server's name, logo, or description changes, remote instances that have the Group actor cached continue showing the old values. Same pattern as the actor-profile federation (Update(Actor)) but the audience is Group followers instead of friends.

Fix: when a server admin updates the Group actor's display name, properties.logoUrl, or summary via PUT /servers/:id, enqueue an Update(Actor) to every remote follower's inbox. The receiving instance's handleUpdate already handles Person and Group actor updates and overwrites the cached row.

The missing piece is just the outbound trigger — the inbound handler is already wired from the profile-updates work.

items.title: Federate calendar events (Create, Update, Delete, RSVP)
items.description:
Calendar events on a server are invisible to remote members. When alice creates, edits, or deletes an event on Test Server, bob on instance B has no idea — his calendar view for the remote server is empty.

Scope:
- Outbound: when a server event is created/updated/deleted, deliver a Create/Update/Delete(Event) activity to the Group's remote followers. The Event object carries title, description, startAt, endAt, location, rrule, slug.
- Inbound: new handler for Event-typed objects that creates/updates/deletes a local shadow event row in the events table with the remote server as owner.
- RSVP federation: when bob RSVPs from B, deliver the RSVP to the origin Group's inbox. The origin updates the attendee list and relays the updated counts to other followers.
- The event embed system ([[event:slug]]) should work cross-instance once the event slug is carried in the federated payload, same pattern as message slugs.

Medium scope — events have more fields than messages and the RSVP relay adds a round-trip, but the delivery infrastructure is the same.

items.title: Federate wiki pages (Create, Update, Delete)
items.description:
Wiki pages on a federated server are invisible to remote members. Bob can see channels and messages but the wiki panel for a remote server is empty.

Scope:
- Outbound: when a wiki page is created/updated/deleted, deliver a Create/Update/Delete(Article) activity to the Group's remote followers. The Article object carries title, slug, content, tags.
- Inbound: new handler for Article-typed objects that creates/updates/deletes a local shadow wiki_pages row. The shadow page's server_id points at the cached remote Group actor.
- The wiki translation pipeline should work transparently on shadow pages since it operates on content, not origin.
- Bidirectional links ([[page-slug]]) would resolve locally against the shadow pages. Cross-instance wiki backlinks are a stretch goal.
- Wiki page revisions could be carried as additional metadata on the Update activity, but a simpler v1 just overwrites the shadow page's content on each Update.

Medium-to-large scope. The wiki data model (pages, revisions, links, tags) is richer than messages, and the translation interaction needs testing. But the underlying delivery and shadow-object patterns are the same as channels.

items.title: Server Files — shared file library with translated descriptions and comment threads
items.description:
A per-server file library that acts as a shared drive — upload files, organize them, add descriptions and comments, and reference them anywhere via the embed system. Think a lightweight FTP/SharePoint document library native to the chat platform, with Babelr's translation layer threaded through every text surface.

This should land BEFORE wiki-attachments because it establishes the file-as-first-class-entity model that wiki attachments, calendar event attachments, and message file references all build on. Instead of each surface having its own upload pipeline and orphan-cleanup story, everything points at the same file library, and the embed system ties it together.

Data model:
- server_files table: id, server_id (FK actors), uploader_id (FK actors), filename, content_type, size_bytes, storage_url, slug (10-char for [[file:slug]] embeds), title, description (translatable), tags, folder_path (nullable, for basic hierarchy), created_at, updated_at
- server_file_comments table: id, file_id (FK server_files), author_id (FK actors), content (translatable), created_at. Reuses the same translation pipeline as messages.
- Partial unique index on slug, same pattern as message/event slugs

Server endpoints:
- GET /servers/:id/files — list files, filterable by folder, tags, content_type
- POST /servers/:id/files — upload (multipart), generates slug, extracts metadata
- GET /servers/:id/files/:fileId — single file metadata + comment thread
- PUT /servers/:id/files/:fileId — update title, description, tags, folder
- DELETE /servers/:id/files/:fileId — remove file + storage cleanup
- POST /servers/:id/files/:fileId/comments — add comment to file
- GET /files/by-slug/:slug — embed lookup (same pattern as messages/events)

Embed system integration:
- [[file:slug]] renders an inline file card: icon by content type, filename, size, description preview, download button
- Images render with a thumbnail preview in the card
- PDFs show page count if available
- The file card links to the full file detail view (description, comments, metadata) in the same inline-view pattern as wiki/calendar
- WikiRefKind extended with 'file', parseWikiRefs recognizes the file: prefix
- extractFileSlugs helper for any future link-tracking

Client UI:
- FilesPanel (inline view, same pattern as WikiPanel/EventsPanel): folder tree sidebar, file grid/list, upload dropzone, file detail view with comments
- FileEmbed component: inline card with type icon, name, size, translated description preview, download button
- FileDetailPanel: full metadata, translated description, comment thread (reuses MessageList/MessageInput for comments), version history (if we add it)
- Upload progress with drag-and-drop anywhere in the files view
- Bulk upload support

Translation integration:
- File descriptions flow through useEventTranslation-style hook (content-hash keyed, same cache layer)
- File comments translate via the existing message translation pipeline (they're structurally identical to messages)
- File titles probably don't need translation (they're usually technical names) but the option is there

Permissions:
- New permissions: VIEW_FILES, UPLOAD_FILES, MANAGE_FILES (delete others' files, edit metadata)
- @everyone gets VIEW_FILES + UPLOAD_FILES by default
- MANAGE_FILES is Moderator+ tier
- Per-folder permissions are a stretch goal (defer until there's demand)

Storage:
- Reuses the existing /uploads storage backend
- Per-server storage quota configurable by admins
- Storage metrics in server settings panel

Federation:
- File metadata (title, description, tags, slug) federated via Create/Update/Delete activities to Group followers
- File binary served from origin via the storage URL (same cross-origin pattern as avatars)
- Remote members see the file library via shadow server_files rows, same pattern as shadow channels
- File comments federate as Note objects with context pointing at the file

Why before wiki-attachments:
- Wiki attachments become 'attach a server file to this page' instead of a separate upload-per-page model
- Calendar event attachments become 'attach a server file to this event'
- Message file sharing becomes 'reference a server file via [[file:slug]]'
- One upload, one storage lifecycle, one permission model, multiple surfaces
- The wiki-attachments roadmap item's scope shrinks dramatically: it becomes 'add a file picker to the wiki editor that inserts [[file:slug]] refs' instead of building a whole parallel upload/storage/cleanup pipeline

Shipping order:
1. Data model + CRUD endpoints + slug generation
2. FilesPanel UI with upload, list, detail view
3. FileEmbed component + renderWithEmbeds integration
4. Comment threads on files
5. Translation for descriptions + comments
6. Federation (shadow files on remote instances)
7. Wiki/calendar/message integration (file picker inserts [[file:slug]])

items.title: Project management — first plugin, reference implementation for embed-plugin-system
items.description:
A native project management surface inside Babelr — work items (tasks/issues/stories), kanban boards, sprint planning — that threads through the same translation and embed ecosystem as everything else. The pitch: a multilingual team's project board where the ticket descriptions, comments, and status updates all translate automatically, and any work item can be referenced from a chat message, wiki page, or calendar event via [[task:slug]].

This is what completes the 'connected workspace' story from the README. Chat + wiki + calendar + files + project management, all cross-referencing each other via the embed fabric, all translating through the same pipeline, all federating across instances. No other platform offers this combination.

Data model:
- project_boards table: id, server_id, name, description (translatable), board_type ('kanban' | 'sprint' | 'backlog'), created_by_id, slug, created_at, updated_at
- board_columns table: id, board_id, name, position, wip_limit (nullable), color
- work_items table: id, board_id, column_id, slug (10-char for [[task:slug]] embeds), title, description (translatable, markdown), assignee_ids (uuid[]), reporter_id, priority ('critical' | 'high' | 'medium' | 'low'), item_type ('task' | 'bug' | 'story' | 'epic'), status, story_points (nullable), tags, due_date (nullable), position (for ordering within column), created_at, updated_at
- work_item_comments table: same shape as server_file_comments — id, work_item_id, author_id, content (translatable), created_at
- sprint table (for sprint boards): id, board_id, name, goal (translatable), start_date, end_date, status ('planning' | 'active' | 'completed')

Embed system:
- [[task:slug]] renders an inline work item card: title, status badge, assignee avatar(s), priority indicator, board name
- Clicking the embed opens the work item detail in the inline view (same pattern as wiki/calendar/files)
- WikiRefKind extended with 'task', parseWikiRefs recognizes the task: prefix
- A wiki page for a feature spec can embed the tasks that implement it; a calendar sprint-planning event can embed the sprint backlog; a chat message can reference a bug report

Client UI:
- ProjectPanel (inline view): board selector, kanban columns with drag-and-drop cards, sprint view with velocity chart
- WorkItemDetail: title, description (markdown with embeds), assignee picker, priority/type selectors, comment thread, activity log, linked items
- Board settings: columns, WIP limits, sprint configuration
- Sidebar entry alongside Calendar and Wiki

Translation:
- Work item titles and descriptions translate via the same content-hash-keyed pipeline
- Comments translate like messages
- Column names and sprint goals translate
- Board names translate
- Status labels are system-defined (not translatable) to avoid semantic drift — 'Done' must mean 'Done' regardless of language

Permissions:
- New permissions: VIEW_BOARDS, CREATE_WORK_ITEMS, MANAGE_BOARDS (create/delete boards, manage columns)
- @everyone gets VIEW_BOARDS + CREATE_WORK_ITEMS by default
- Assignment doesn't require permission — anyone who can view can be assigned

Federation:
- Work items federate as custom AP objects to Group followers
- Remote members see the board and can create/update/comment on items
- Sprint state syncs across instances
- The project board is the strongest 'cross-company collaboration' story: two companies working on a joint project, each on their own Babelr instance, sharing a kanban board where every card translates into each team's language

Relationship to external tools:
- This is intentionally simpler than Jira/Linear — it's a built-in lightweight tracker, not a full PM suite
- The embed-plugin-system roadmap item would let someone build a Jira/Linear/ADO plugin that renders [[jira:PROJ-123]] alongside native [[task:slug]] items — both in the same embed fabric
- Import from external PM tools (Jira CSV, Linear API, GitHub Issues) is a follow-up, same pattern as wiki-import

Shipping order:
1. Data model + board/column/item CRUD endpoints + slug generation
2. Kanban board UI with drag-and-drop
3. Work item detail view with comments
4. TaskEmbed component + renderWithEmbeds integration
5. Translation for descriptions + comments
6. Sprint planning view (sprint boards, velocity)
7. Federation
8. Cross-surface integration (wiki embeds, calendar links, chat references)

--- IMPLEMENTATION NOTE ---

This item is now coupled with embed-plugin-system: PM ships AS the first plugin, not as hardcoded first-party code. The plugin system infrastructure lands first (manifest format, kind registry, client lazy-loading, server route prefix, @babelr/plugin-sdk), then PM is built entirely through the plugin boundary in packages/plugins/project-management/.

If something requires reaching outside the plugin API to touch core code, that is a signal the API is missing a capability — fix the API, not the plugin. This validates the plugin system with a non-trivial real-world workload before third parties try to use it.

Calendars, wikis, and files remain first-party (hardcoded) because they are generally useful on every instance. Project management is the first specialized surface — the right place to prove the plugin model.

The shipping order becomes:
1. embed-plugin-system infrastructure (see that item)
2. PM plugin built through the API (this item)
3. Third-party plugins (ADO, Jira, etc.) use the same API the PM plugin validated

--- UPDATE ---

Polls & Quizzes (see plugin-polls-quizzes) will be the FIRST plugin scaffold, not PM. PM moves to second plugin. Polls are smaller, validate the interactive-embed pattern (inline voting), and surface plugin API gaps before PM makes them expensive. The chat input refactor (embed insertion menu) also ships with the polls plugin since polls are the first content type that users CREATE from the message input via the plugin system.

items.title: Federate server files to remote instances
items.description:
File metadata (title, description, tags, slug, storageUrl) should federate via Create/Update/Delete activities to Group followers, same pattern as channel messages. Remote members see the file library via shadow server_files rows. File binaries served from origin via storageUrl (cross-origin, same as avatars). File chat comments federate automatically since they use the message pipeline, but the file metadata itself (create/update/delete) needs explicit activity delivery.

Also: the GET /files/by-slug/:slug endpoint needs the same origin-proxy fallback as messages/by-slug for cross-instance [[file:slug]] embeds.

items.title: Per-server storage quotas and usage metrics for files
items.description: Admins should be able to set a storage quota per server (e.g. 5 GB) and see current usage in the server settings panel. Upload should reject with a clear error when the quota would be exceeded. Storage metrics: total files, total size, breakdown by content type. Orphaned file cleanup (storage objects with no server_files row) as a background sweep.

items.title: Drag-and-drop file upload + bulk upload
items.description: The current upload flow uses a hidden file input triggered by a button click. Add drag-and-drop onto the file list area (dropzone with visual feedback), clipboard paste for images (same pattern as message attachments), and multi-file selection for bulk uploads with a progress indicator per file.

items.title: Wiki attachments as server file references
items.description:
Now that server-files exists, wiki-attachments becomes: add a file picker to the wiki editor that inserts [[file:slug]] refs, plus drag-and-drop image paste that auto-uploads to the server file library and inserts the embed. The wiki-attachments roadmap item's original scope (separate upload pipeline, per-page storage, orphan cleanup) is no longer needed — everything points at the shared file library.

Scope:
- File picker button in the wiki edit toolbar that opens a modal showing the server's file library (reuse FilesPanel or a stripped-down variant)
- Selecting a file inserts [[file:slug]] at the cursor
- Drag-and-drop image onto the editor auto-uploads and inserts the embed
- Clipboard paste (Ctrl+V with an image) does the same
- Images from [[file:slug]] embeds render inline in wiki view mode (the FileEmbed already handles this with thumbnails; may want a larger inline render for wiki context)

items.title: Wiki page comment threads via the message pipeline
items.description:
Wiki pages should have comment threads, same pattern as event chat and file comments — each page gets an OrderedCollection on creation, and the comment thread uses useChat + MessageList + MessageInput. Reactions, threads, typing indicators, translation, and federation all come for free.

Scope:
- Add chat_id column to wiki_pages (FK to objects, same as events.event_chat_id and server_files.chat_id)
- Create the OrderedCollection on page creation
- WikiPanel renders the comment thread alongside the page content (below the content or in a side panel)
- Delete cleans up the chat collection (cascade handles messages)
- Migration + backfill for existing pages (create empty collections)

This is a well-proven pattern at this point — events and files both use it. The implementation is mostly copy-paste from the file comments refactor.

items.title: Wiki revision history viewer with restore functionality
items.description:
The wiki_page_revisions table already tracks every edit (revision_number, title, content, edited_by, summary, timestamp) — it's been populated since day one but there's no UI to view or restore revisions.

Scope:
- Revision list view: show all revisions for a page with revision number, editor name, timestamp, and optional summary. Clickable to view that revision's content.
- Diff view: side-by-side or inline diff between any two revisions (or between a revision and the current content). Use a lightweight diff library or compute on the server.
- Restore button: restores a previous revision's content by creating a new revision with the old content (not by deleting history). The restore itself is just an update with content = selectedRevision.content.
- Translation: revision content translates through the existing wiki translation pipeline when viewed.
- Federation: revision history is local to each instance — the origin tracks the canonical history, remote shadows just see the latest content. Restoring a revision on the origin triggers a normal Update(Article) that federates.

Endpoints:
- GET /servers/:serverId/wiki/pages/:slug/revisions — list revisions (already partially exists as revision data is written, just needs the read endpoint)
- GET /servers/:serverId/wiki/pages/:slug/revisions/:revisionNumber — single revision content
- POST /servers/:serverId/wiki/pages/:slug/restore/:revisionNumber — convenience endpoint that updates content to the specified revision

Client:
- Revision list panel (sidebar or dropdown in the wiki view header)
- Revision viewer (read-only render of old content)
- Diff viewer (stretch goal — start with just viewing old versions and restoring)
- 'Restored from revision N' summary auto-populated on restore

This is critical for any real wiki use — the ability to see who changed what and roll back mistakes is what makes a wiki trustworthy for shared documentation.

items.title: Polls & Quizzes plugin — first plugin scaffold before Project Management
items.description:
The project-management plugin was designated as the reference implementation for the embed-plugin-system, but PM is a large surface (kanban boards, sprints, drag-and-drop, work item CRUD) that conflates plugin API validation with feature complexity. A simpler first plugin validates the plugin system infrastructure without the PM scope.

Polls & Quizzes is the right size:
- Small data model: poll/quiz with options, votes per user, optional correct answers for quizzes
- Interactive embed: [[poll:slug]] renders inline with vote buttons that work directly from the embed — no navigation needed. First truly interactive embed beyond the event RSVP buttons.
- Translatable: poll questions and option labels flow through the translation pipeline
- Composable: polls can be embedded in chat messages, wiki pages, calendar event descriptions, file descriptions — anywhere that accepts text
- Federation-ready: poll state (votes, results) can sync across instances via the same activity delivery pattern

Why before PM:
- Proves the plugin manifest, kind registration, client component contract, and server route prefix work end-to-end with a real but simple feature
- The interactive embed pattern (voting inline without navigating away) is new — event RSVP is the closest precedent but polls have multiple options, vote counts, and optional reveal-after-vote semantics
- Forces the plugin SDK to handle a lifecycle the current embeds don't: state changes (voting) that need to propagate to all mounted instances of the same embed in real time (same pub/sub cache pattern as EventEmbed's RSVP sync)
- Surfaces any gaps in the plugin API before PM makes them expensive to fix

Also motivates refactoring the chat message input:
- The current 'Attach file' button is a standalone upload flow. With polls, the input needs a more general 'insert content' affordance — attach a file, create a poll, reference an event, link a wiki page. The embed architecture already supports all of these via [[kind:slug]]; the input UX should make them discoverable.
- A '+' or '/' command menu in the message input that lists available embed kinds (built-in + plugins) and walks the user through creating/selecting content to embed. The plugin manifest declares what each plugin contributes to this menu.

Shipping order:
1. Plugin system infrastructure (manifest, registry, route prefix, client lazy-load) — from embed-plugin-system item
2. Polls & Quizzes as the first plugin in packages/plugins/polls/
3. Interactive embed with inline voting + real-time vote sync
4. Chat input refactor: '+' menu for inserting embeds (files, polls, events, wiki refs)
5. Then PM plugin builds on the proven infrastructure

items.title: Refactor wiki refs to [[wiki:slug]] and design cross-server embed addressing
items.description:
Wiki page refs currently use bare [[page-slug]] syntax (no prefix), while every other embed kind uses [[kind:slug]]. This inconsistency means wiki refs are the fallthrough default in the parser rather than an explicit kind, which will get awkward when the plugin system allows arbitrary kind registration — a plugin that happens to produce slugs that look like wiki page slugs would collide.

Phase 1 — prefix consistency:
- Add 'wiki:' as a recognized prefix in parseWikiRefs, producing kind='page' refs just like the current bare-slug path
- Keep bare [[slug]] working as a backwards-compatible alias for [[wiki:slug]] so existing content doesn't break
- New content created via UI affordances (wiki link picker, copy-reference buttons) should emit [[wiki:slug]] format
- Migration path: existing wiki content with bare [[slug]] refs continues to work indefinitely; no mass rewrite needed

Phase 2 — cross-server embed addressing:
- Extend the ref syntax to support [[server@tower:kind:slug]] for referencing content on a specific remote server
- Example: [[test-server@partner.com:wiki:onboarding]] would resolve to the 'onboarding' wiki page on the 'test-server' Group at babelr-a.local
- The parser extracts the server handle, the embed component resolves it via the federation proxy (signed GET to the origin), and the rendered embed shows the remote content with a visual indicator of which instance it came from
- This turns every embed into a federation-aware cross-reference: a wiki page on one Tower can embed a poll from another Tower, a calendar event from a third, and a file from a fourth — all translating into the reader's language

This is the final piece that makes the [[kind:slug]] fabric truly universal: not just cross-surface, but cross-instance. The addressing syntax is the API surface that plugins and users both interact with, so getting it right before the plugin system ships is important.

items.title: Federation access control — instance allowlists, server visibility, and data sharing policies
items.description:
Currently every instance is open: any remote instance can discover actors via WebFinger, follow Groups, fetch channels/members/wiki/events/files via the AP collection endpoints, and deliver activities to inboxes. Enterprise deployments need granular control over who can interact with their data.

Three layers, each independently configurable:

## Layer 1: Instance-level federation policy

Admin-configured in a new instance settings panel (or via env vars for initial setup).

- **Federation mode**: `open` (default, current behavior), `allowlist` (only listed domains can interact), `closed` (no federation — single-tenant mode for air-gapped deployments)
- **Domain allowlist**: when mode is `allowlist`, a list of domains (e.g. `partner.com`, `vendor.babelr.cloud`) that are permitted to send activities, resolve actors, and fetch collections. All other domains get 403 on inbox POSTs, WebFinger, and collection endpoints.
- **Domain blocklist**: when mode is `open`, a list of domains to reject. Useful for blocking a specific bad actor without switching to full allowlist mode.
- **Auto-accept follows**: whether Group Follow requests from allowed domains are auto-accepted (current behavior) or require admin approval. Default: auto-accept for allowed domains.

Enforcement points:
- WebFinger: reject resource lookups from blocked/unlisted domains (check the requesting IP or require signed requests)
- Inbox POST: verify the signing actor's domain against the policy before processing the activity
- Collection endpoints (/groups/:slug/channels, /members, /wiki/pages, /events, /files): check the requesting domain (via HTTP Signature or referrer) against the policy
- Delivery queue: don't enqueue activities to inboxes on blocked/unlisted domains

## Layer 2: Server-level visibility

Per-server settings, configured by the server admin.

- **Server visibility**: `public` (discoverable via WebFinger and the discover endpoint), `unlisted` (not in discover results but accessible if you have the handle), `private` (only accessible via invite — the server's Group actor returns 404 to non-members)
- **Federation scope**: `all` (federate with any allowed instance), `specific` (only federate with a manually-curated list of partner instances for this specific server). This lets a company have one public community server and one private internal server on the same instance.
- **Content sharing policy**: which content types remote members can access. E.g. allow channel messages but not wiki pages or files. Enforced at the collection endpoint level.

## Layer 3: Data residency and content controls

For regulated industries (healthcare, finance, government).

- **Content classification**: admins can tag channels, wiki pages, or files as 'internal' or 'confidential'. Internal content is excluded from federation delivery entirely — it never leaves the instance, even for members who joined from a remote server.
- **Attachment policy**: whether file binaries are served to remote instances (current: yes, via cross-origin storageUrl) or whether remote members only see metadata with a 'request access' button. Useful when file content is sensitive but the file library's organizational structure isn't.
- **Audit trail**: log every federation interaction (who fetched what from which instance, when). Ties into the audit-logs roadmap item.

## Implementation approach

New tables:
- `federation_policies`: instance-level settings (mode, allowlist, blocklist, auto-accept)
- `server_federation_settings`: per-server overrides (visibility, federation scope, content sharing policy)

Enforcement middleware:
- A Fastify hook that runs before every federation-facing endpoint (inbox routes, collection routes, WebFinger). Checks the requesting domain against the policy. Cached in memory with a short TTL so policy changes take effect quickly.
- The delivery queue checks the target domain against the policy before attempting delivery. Blocked deliveries are silently dropped (not retried).

Admin UI:
- Instance Settings → Federation panel: mode selector, domain allowlist/blocklist editor, auto-accept toggle
- Server Settings → Federation tab: visibility selector, federation scope, content sharing checkboxes

## What this enables

- A hospital runs a closed Babelr instance for internal communication — zero federation, complete data isolation
- A consulting firm runs an allowlist instance that federates only with their three client companies' instances
- A company has a public community server for open-source contributors and a private internal server for employees, both on the same instance
- A defense contractor tags certain channels as 'confidential' — those channels' messages never leave the instance even though the server is federated with partner organizations
- An admin can see exactly which remote instance accessed which wiki page and when

This is the trust infrastructure that makes enterprise buyers comfortable with federation. Without it, federation is a liability ('our data is accessible to anyone who runs a Babelr instance'). With it, federation is a controlled capability that IT can scope, audit, and revoke.

items.title: Babelr MCP server — AI agents as first-class Tower citizens
items.description:
An MCP (Model Context Protocol) server that exposes Babelr's full surface area to AI agents. This is two things at once: a developer tool for using Babelr data in AI-assisted workflows, and an integration layer that makes AI agents first-class participants in Towers.

As a developer tool:
- AI coding assistants (Claude Code, Cursor, Copilot) can read and write to Babelr directly via MCP
- Query wiki pages for context while coding: 'What does the API spec say about rate limiting?'
- Search messages for decisions: 'When did we decide to switch to PostgreSQL?'
- Create wiki pages from conversation summaries
- Reference Babelr content via [[server@tower:kind:slug]] in commit messages, PR descriptions, and docs — the AI can resolve and inline the content
- File operations: upload build artifacts, read shared design assets
- Calendar awareness: 'What meetings do I have today?' 'Create an event for the sprint review'

As an AI agent integration layer:
- AI agents can be configured as actors on a Tower — they get an actor profile, can join servers, post in channels, respond to mentions, and participate in conversations
- A server admin can add an AI agent to their server that: answers questions from the wiki, summarizes long threads, translates on demand, triages bug reports into the project management board, monitors channels for action items
- Agents interact through the same API surface as human users — they see the same permissions, the same content, the same embed system. An agent can post a [[wiki:summary]] embed just like a human can.
- Agent configuration UI: per-server, admins pick which agent to activate, configure its system prompt, set which channels it monitors, what permissions it has
- The translation pipeline means an agent can be configured in one language and respond in each user's preferred language automatically

MCP server shape:

Resources (read):
- babelr://servers — list servers the agent has access to
- babelr://servers/{id}/channels — channel list
- babelr://servers/{id}/channels/{id}/messages — message history
- babelr://servers/{id}/wiki/pages — wiki page list
- babelr://servers/{id}/wiki/pages/{slug} — single page content
- babelr://servers/{id}/events — calendar events
- babelr://servers/{id}/files — file library
- babelr://dms — DM conversations

Tools (write):
- send_message(channelId, content) — post a message
- create_wiki_page(serverId, title, content) — create a wiki page
- update_wiki_page(serverId, slug, content) — edit a page
- create_event(serverId, title, startAt, endAt, ...) — create a calendar event
- upload_file(serverId, file, metadata) — upload to the file library
- search_messages(query, channelId?) — full-text search
- resolve_embed(server, tower, kind, slug) — resolve a cross-tower embed

Prompts (templates):
- summarize_thread(channelId, messageId) — summarize a thread
- draft_wiki_page(topic, context) — draft a wiki page from context
- translate_content(content, targetLang) — on-demand translation

Authentication:
- The MCP server authenticates as a specific actor on the Tower (API key or session token)
- For developer-tool use: authenticates as the developer's own actor
- For agent use: authenticates as a dedicated agent actor with its own permissions

Package:
- packages/mcp-server/ in the monorepo
- Published as @babelr/mcp-server on npm
- Configurable via claude_desktop_config.json, .cursor/mcp.json, etc.
- Can point at any Tower (local dev, staging, production)

Why this matters:
- Every Tower becomes an AI-accessible knowledge base. The wiki, the message history, the file library, the calendar — all queryable by agents.
- The embed system means AI-generated content seamlessly references human-authored content and vice versa. An agent can write a wiki page that embeds [[msg:slug]] refs to the chat messages it summarized.
- Federation means agents on one Tower can reference content on other Towers via [[server@tower:kind:slug]] — a coding agent can pull in the API spec from the engineering Tower while summarizing a bug report from the support Tower.
- The translation pipeline means a single agent configuration works for multilingual teams without any per-language setup.

items.title: Wiki page hierarchy — parent-child nesting, sidebar tree, breadcrumbs
items.description:
Pages are currently flat — a list sorted by recency with no structural relationship between them. Mature wikis have nested pages (a page can be a child of another page), which gives the wiki a navigable tree structure.

Scope:
- Add parent_id (nullable FK to wiki_pages.id) column to wiki_pages
- Sidebar renders as a collapsible tree instead of a flat list
- Breadcrumb trail shows the page's ancestry (Home > Engineering > API > Rate Limiting)
- Page ordering within a sibling group (position integer, drag-to-reorder)
- Table of contents auto-generated from headings within a page
- 'Move page' action to reparent a page in the tree

Critical for import: Confluence, Notion, BookStack, and Obsidian all have hierarchical page structures. Without parent-child support, imported content loses its organizational structure and becomes a flat soup of pages.

Also needed for the project wiki dogfooding — architectural docs need to nest (Federation > Delivery Queue > Retry Logic).

items.title: Wiki page templates — meeting notes, decision records, RFCs, runbooks
items.description:
No template system exists. When creating a page, the user gets a blank editor. Templates provide starter content for common page types.

Scope:
- Template library per server (stored as wiki_page_templates table or as specially-tagged pages)
- 'New from template' in the create page flow — shows template picker
- Built-in templates shipped with every server: Meeting Notes, Decision Record (ADR), RFC, Runbook, Onboarding Guide
- Custom templates: any page can be saved as a template
- Templates are just markdown content with placeholder tokens ({{date}}, {{author}}, {{title}})
- Templates translate — the template content flows through the translation pipeline so a Japanese user sees the Meeting Notes template in Japanese

Important for adoption — templates reduce the 'blank page' friction that stops people from writing docs.

items.title: Full-text search across wiki page content
items.description:
Currently the sidebar only filters by title/slug substring (client-side). No server-side full-text search of page content exists.

Scope:
- Add a content_search tsvector column to wiki_pages (same pattern as objects.content_search for messages)
- Populate via trigger or on insert/update
- GET /servers/:serverId/wiki/search?q=query endpoint with ranked results
- Search UI in the wiki sidebar with content preview snippets and highlighted matches
- Tag-based filtering in the search API (q=query&tag=architecture)

Note: the message search infrastructure already exists (objects.content_search with GIN index + to_tsquery). Wiki search can reuse the same pattern.

items.title: Wiki content enhancements — syntax highlighting, callouts, task lists, ToC
items.description:
The markdown renderer supports basic formatting but lacks features that make wiki content rich and scannable.

Scope:
- Syntax-highlighted code blocks via highlight.js or Prism (language detection from the fenced block tag)
- Callout/admonition blocks (> [!NOTE], > [!WARNING], > [!TIP] — GitHub-flavored)
- Task lists (- [ ] / - [x] checkboxes, interactive toggle)
- Auto-generated table of contents from headings (insertable via a [[toc]] directive or always-visible sidebar)
- Footnotes
- Mermaid diagram rendering (stretch goal — adds a large dependency)

The syntax highlighting is the most impactful — any engineering wiki without it looks amateur.

items.title: Wiki navigation — recently edited, all pages, orphaned pages, page stats
items.description:
The sidebar shows a flat list with tag filter and search. Missing the discovery features that help users find and maintain content.

Scope:
- 'Recently edited' section showing the last N pages modified (with editor name and timestamp)
- 'All pages' alphabetical index view
- 'Orphaned pages' — pages with no backlinks (nothing links to them), surfaced so admins can either link them or delete them
- Page statistics: view count (requires a page_views table), edit count (already available from revisions), last editor
- 'Related pages' suggestions based on shared tags or backlink graph proximity

items.title: Wiki export — markdown bundle, PDF, HTML
items.description:
No export capability exists. Users need to be able to extract their wiki content in portable formats.

Scope:
- Export single page as markdown (.md file download)
- Export single page as PDF (server-side rendering via Puppeteer or a lightweight HTML-to-PDF library)
- Export entire wiki as a zip of markdown files (preserving folder structure from page hierarchy)
- Export preserves [[wiki:slug]] refs as relative links between files
- Export includes embedded images as local files with rewritten paths

Critical for enterprise: procurement teams ask 'can we get our data out?' and the answer must be yes.
